cloud.title: Linux & Networking Foundations
cloud.description: Cloud engineering starts with Linux and networking fluency. Everything you deploy runs on Linux. You need to navigate servers, understand networking primitives, and automate with shell scripts.
topics: Linux file system, permissions, users & groups
Shell scripting — variables, loops, functions, cron
Networking — IP addressing, subnets, CIDR, routing
SSH — key-based auth, tunneling, bastion hosts
cloud.challenge: Provision an Ubuntu VPS (AWS free tier, DigitalOcean, or similar), set up a non-root user with SSH key auth, configure ufw firewall, and deploy a simple Node.js app as a systemd service.

children.title: File System & Permissions
children.description: Understanding the Linux file system hierarchy and permission model (owner/group/other, rwx bits, setuid) is non-negotiable for cloud work. Every misconfigured permission is a potential security hole or broken deployment that manifests silently and unpredictably.

children.title: systemd & Service Management
children.description: systemd is the init system on every modern Linux server you will provision in the cloud. Without it you cannot manage long-running processes, debug startup failures, or set up health-checked services that survive reboots.

children.title: IP Addressing & CIDR
children.description: Cloud networking is built entirely on IP addressing and CIDR notation. Misunderstanding subnetting leads to overlapping VPC ranges, routing black holes, and security group rules that never match the traffic you intend.

children.title: SSH & Bastion Hosts
children.description: SSH is the primary administration channel for cloud VMs. Key-pair mismanagement or a misconfigured bastion host is the most common way engineers lock themselves out of production or leave an open pivot point for attackers.

children.title: Bash Scripting Fundamentals
children.description: Bash scripts are the glue layer of cloud automation — from user-data bootstrap scripts to cron-driven maintenance jobs. A fragile script without proper error handling will silently corrupt state during a 3 AM auto-scaling event.

children.title: Cron & Scheduled Tasks
children.description: Cron is universally available on Linux and is the simplest mechanism for periodic cloud jobs such as log rotation, backup snapshots, and certificate renewal. A mistyped cron expression quietly skips critical jobs with no alerting by default.

children.title: DNS Fundamentals
children.description: DNS is how every piece of your cloud infrastructure resolves names to addresses. Misunderstanding TTLs during a failover leads to users hitting dead endpoints for hours even after you have promoted a new IP.

children.title: Reverse Proxies with Nginx
children.description: Nginx acts as the front door for most cloud deployments — handling TLS termination, load balancing, and rate limiting before traffic ever reaches your application. An incorrectly tuned worker or buffer setting is the silent killer of high-concurrency workloads.

cloud.title: AWS Core Services
cloud.description: AWS is the dominant cloud platform. Learn the foundational services — compute, storage, networking, and databases — that underpin almost every production architecture.
topics: IAM — users, roles, policies, least-privilege principle
VPC — subnets, security groups, NACLs, internet gateways
EC2 — instance types, AMIs, key pairs, user data scripts
S3 — buckets, objects, policies, versioning, lifecycle rules

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — AWS Full Course
A Cloud Guru — AWS Solutions Architect

cloud.challenge: Deploy a full-stack app on AWS — EC2 with Nginx, RDS PostgreSQL, S3 for static assets, proper IAM roles (no root credentials), and a VPC with public/private subnets.

children.title: IAM Roles & Least Privilege
children.description: IAM roles are the identity fabric of AWS — every EC2 instance, Lambda function, and pipeline job should assume a role rather than use long-lived credentials. Over-permissive roles are the number-one cause of cloud data breaches and are trivially exploited once an instance is compromised.

children.title: VPC Architecture: Subnets & Security Groups
children.description: VPCs define the network boundary of your AWS workloads. Placing databases in public subnets or writing overly permissive security group rules destroys the defense-in-depth model and exposes internal services to the internet.

children.title: EC2 Instance Types & Pricing
children.description: Choosing the wrong instance family (compute-optimized vs memory-optimized vs burstable) results in either wasted spend or throttled performance at scale. Understanding the difference between on-demand, reserved, and spot pricing is essential for any production budget.

children.title: Auto Scaling Groups & Load Balancers
children.description: Auto Scaling Groups combined with an Application Load Balancer are the backbone of horizontally scalable, self-healing architectures on AWS. A misconfigured health check threshold can trigger cascading terminations during a brief spike, pulling healthy instances offline.

children.title: S3 Bucket Policies & Access Control
children.description: S3 is infinitely scalable but its access model is notoriously complex — bucket policies, ACLs, and Block Public Access all interact in non-obvious ways. A single misconfigured policy has exposed entire company databases to the public internet.

children.title: S3 Versioning & Lifecycle Rules
children.description: Versioning protects against accidental deletes and overwrites, while lifecycle rules automatically transition objects to cheaper storage tiers. Without lifecycle rules, forgotten log buckets grow indefinitely and generate surprise bills.

children.title: Amazon RDS: Multi-AZ & Backups
children.description: RDS automates patching, backups, and failover but only if you configure Multi-AZ and automated snapshots correctly. A single-AZ RDS instance with default retention is one failed disk away from hours of downtime and permanent data loss.

children.title: CloudWatch Alarms & Monitoring
children.description: CloudWatch is the native observability layer for all AWS services. Without properly configured alarms and dashboards you are operating blind — you will learn about an outage from an angry customer rather than a PagerDuty notification.

cloud.title: Infrastructure as Code — Terraform
cloud.description: Stop clicking around in the console. Define your infrastructure in code, version it with Git, and deploy reproducible environments with Terraform.
topics: HCL syntax — blocks, arguments, expressions, locals
Core workflow — init, plan, apply, destroy
Remote state with S3 + DynamoDB locking

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — Terraform Full Course

cloud.challenge: Rewrite your AWS infrastructure from the previous challenge in Terraform — VPC, EC2, RDS, S3, IAM, remote state — all reproducible from a single terraform apply.

children.title: Providers, Resources & State
children.description: Terraform state is the source of truth about what infrastructure actually exists. If state becomes corrupted or out of sync — through manual console changes or concurrent applies — Terraform will attempt to recreate or destroy live resources, causing outages.

children.title: Variables, Outputs & Locals
children.description: Variables and outputs are the interface contract of a Terraform module. Hardcoding values instead of parameterizing them creates brittle configurations that cannot be reused across environments and forces dangerous copy-paste sprawl.

children.title: S3 Backend with DynamoDB Locking
children.description: A remote backend with state locking prevents two engineers — or two CI jobs — from running terraform apply simultaneously and corrupting the state file. Without locking, concurrent applies produce race conditions that can double-create or destroy production infrastructure.

children.title: Terraform Workspaces
children.description: Workspaces allow a single Terraform configuration to manage multiple isolated environment state files. Without a clear multi-environment strategy, teams end up with duplicated configs or accidentally apply dev changes to production.

children.title: Expressions, Functions & Loops
children.description: Terraform's expression language enables dynamic resource creation with for_each and count, conditional logic, and data transformations. Without understanding these primitives you resort to manually duplicating resource blocks — which breaks the moment requirements change.

children.title: Terraform Modules
children.description: Modules are the unit of reuse in Terraform — they encapsulate a logical group of resources behind a clean interface. A team without a module strategy ships copy-pasted configurations that diverge over time and become impossible to audit for security compliance.

children.title: Plan on PR, Apply on Merge
children.description: Running terraform plan as a pull request check gives reviewers visibility into infrastructure changes before they land in production. Skipping this step means infrastructure changes bypass code review — the engineering equivalent of pushing untested code directly to main.

children.title: Policy as Code with Sentinel
children.description: Sentinel and OPA allow you to enforce organizational guardrails — allowed regions, required tags, encryption mandates — as code that runs during every Terraform plan. Without policy gates, one engineer can inadvertently deploy resources that violate compliance requirements.

cloud.title: Containers with Docker
cloud.description: Containers are the fundamental unit of modern cloud deployment. Master Docker — from writing efficient Dockerfiles to orchestrating multi-service applications.
topics: Container concepts — images, layers, registry, runtime
Dockerfile best practices — multi-stage builds, layer caching
Docker Compose for local multi-service development
AWS ECR — push and pull private images
Container security — rootless, read-only FS, secrets
cloud.challenge: Containerize a multi-service app (frontend + API + database), push to ECR, and update your Terraform to deploy the container on EC2 pulling from ECR.

children.title: Dockerfile Best Practices
children.description: A well-structured Dockerfile with multi-stage builds produces small, reproducible images that start faster and expose a smaller attack surface. Ignoring layer caching order turns a 10-second incremental build into a 10-minute full rebuild on every CI run.

children.title: Container Security
children.description: Running containers as root, mounting host paths, or embedding secrets in image layers are the three most common container security mistakes. A single compromised container running as root can escape to the host and take down the entire fleet.

children.title: Docker Networking Modes
children.description: Docker networking modes (bridge, host, overlay) control how containers communicate with each other and the outside world. Misunderstanding network isolation causes services to be inadvertently exposed or, conversely, unable to reach required dependencies.

children.title: Docker Compose for Local Development
children.description: Docker Compose defines multi-service stacks declaratively, eliminating the 'works on my machine' problem by ensuring every developer and CI runner spins up the exact same database, cache, and message broker versions.

children.title: AWS ECR: Push, Pull & Scanning
children.description: ECR is the private container registry that integrates natively with IAM, ECS, and EKS. Pushing unscanned images to production is how vulnerable base images propagate silently — ECR native scanning catches CVEs before they ever land on a cluster node.

children.title: Image Tagging Strategies
children.description: Using mutable tags like 'latest' in production deployments makes rollbacks nearly impossible and breaks auditability — you lose the ability to tell which code is actually running. Immutable tags tied to git SHAs are the foundation of traceable production deployments.

children.title: Docker Volumes & Bind Mounts
children.description: Volumes persist data beyond the container lifecycle and are the correct mechanism for stateful workloads like databases. Bind-mounting host paths in production is a security risk; volume drivers provide the portability needed for cloud-native storage.

children.title: Secrets Management in Docker
children.description: Embedding secrets as ENV vars in Dockerfiles bakes them into every image layer and exposes them in docker inspect. Docker secrets and external vault integration ensure credentials never appear in build logs, registries, or container metadata.

cloud.title: Kubernetes
cloud.description: Kubernetes is the industry standard for container orchestration at scale. Learn to deploy, scale, and manage containerized workloads on managed clusters.
topics: K8s architecture — control plane, nodes, pods, services
AWS EKS — managed cluster setup, IAM integration

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — Kubernetes Full Course

cloud.challenge: Deploy your containerized app to EKS — write Kubernetes manifests (Deployment, Service, Ingress), package with Helm, and configure autoscaling based on CPU.

children.title: Control Plane & Node Components
children.description: The control plane (API server, etcd, scheduler, controller manager) is the brain of Kubernetes. Without understanding these components you cannot diagnose scheduling failures, API server timeouts, or etcd quorum losses — all of which silently stop new workloads from starting.

children.title: Pods, Deployments & ReplicaSets
children.description: Deployments wrap ReplicaSets to provide declarative rolling updates and rollback capability. Directly creating pods without a controller means they will never restart on failure — a guarantee that breaks the moment a node is evicted or drained.

children.title: ConfigMaps & Secrets
children.description: ConfigMaps and Secrets decouple application configuration from the container image, enabling environment-specific tuning without rebuilding images. Secrets stored as base64 in a public repo are not encrypted — they are just encoded, and attackers know the difference.

children.title: Resource Requests & Limits
children.description: Resource requests inform the Kubernetes scheduler where a pod can fit; limits prevent noisy neighbours from consuming the entire node. Without them, a single misbehaving pod can starve every other workload on a node and trigger cascading evictions.

children.title: AWS EKS Setup & IAM Integration
children.description: EKS offloads control-plane management but requires careful IAM-to-RBAC mapping via IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts). Getting this wrong either locks workloads out of AWS APIs or grants every pod on the cluster full administrator access.

resources.label: EKS IAM Roles for Service Accounts

children.title: Helm Chart Packaging & Release
children.description: Helm templatizes Kubernetes manifests into parameterized packages that can be versioned, diffed, and rolled back with a single command. Without Helm (or a comparable tool), deploying the same app to multiple environments means manually editing YAML — a process that guarantees configuration drift.

children.title: Services: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer
children.description: Services provide stable DNS and load-balanced access to pods whose IP addresses change on every restart. Using pod IPs directly in configuration is a common beginner mistake that causes random connection failures whenever a pod is rescheduled.

children.title: Horizontal Pod Autoscaling
children.description: HPA automatically scales the number of pod replicas based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. Without it, traffic spikes either exhaust a fixed pod count and cause latency spikes, or you overprovision idle replicas around the clock and burn budget unnecessarily.

cloud.title: CI/CD Pipelines
cloud.description: Automate your path from code commit to production deployment. Build pipelines that test, build, scan, and deploy automatically, with GitOps principles for Kubernetes.
topics: GitHub Actions — workflows, jobs, steps, secrets, matrix builds
CI pipeline — lint, test, build Docker image, push to ECR
CD with ArgoCD — GitOps, app sync, health checks
Terraform in CI — plan on PR, apply on merge
Secrets management — GitHub Secrets, AWS Secrets Manager

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana — CI/CD Full Course

cloud.challenge: Build an end-to-end GitOps pipeline — on push: run tests, build and push Docker image to ECR, update Helm values, and let ArgoCD auto-deploy to EKS.

children.title: Workflows, Jobs & Steps
children.description: GitHub Actions workflows define the entire path from code commit to deployed artifact. A poorly structured workflow with no job caching or matrix builds turns a 2-minute CI into a 20-minute bottleneck that slows every developer on the team.

children.title: GitHub Actions Secrets & OIDC
children.description: OIDC federation lets GitHub Actions assume AWS roles without storing long-lived access keys as repository secrets. Long-lived credentials in secrets are rotated only when someone remembers to, and they persist even after the engineer who created them leaves the organization.

children.title: ArgoCD Application Sync & Drift Detection
children.description: ArgoCD continuously compares the live cluster state against the desired state in Git and alerts or auto-corrects on any drift. Without drift detection, manual kubectl changes silently diverge from the documented configuration and create ghost changes that survive the next deployment.

children.title: Blue/Green & Canary Deployments
children.description: Blue/green and canary strategies decouple the release from the deployment, allowing you to route a small percentage of real traffic to new code before committing fully. Without controlled traffic splitting, every deploy is an all-or-nothing bet that a production outage will not occur.

children.title: AWS Secrets Manager & Parameter Store
children.description: Secrets Manager rotates credentials automatically and audit-logs every access through CloudTrail. Applications that read secrets from environment variables baked at build time keep stale credentials long after rotation occurs, silently failing on the first request.

children.title: Terraform in CI: Plan & Apply Gates
children.description: Running terraform plan as a required PR check and terraform apply only after merge creates a mandatory human review point for every infrastructure change. Bypassing this gate — even once — is how accidental resource deletions reach production.

children.title: Docker Build & Push in GitHub Actions
children.description: A well-structured build job uses layer caching to keep image builds fast and pushes immutable, SHa-tagged images to ECR. Rebuilding every layer from scratch on every commit turns a deployable artifact from a 30-second step into a 5-minute pipeline stall.

children.title: Image Scanning in CI (Trivy)
children.description: Scanning container images for CVEs during the build step blocks vulnerable images before they ever reach a registry or cluster. Images that ship without scanning accumulate known critical vulnerabilities that become public liabilities the moment a breach occurs.

cloud.title: Monitoring & Observability
cloud.description: You can't fix what you can't see. Build observability into your stack with metrics, logs, and traces. Know when something breaks before your users tell you.
topics: Three pillars of observability — metrics, logs, traces
cloud.challenge: Instrument your Kubernetes workload with Prometheus metrics, build a Grafana dashboard showing request rate, error rate, and latency, and set up an alert for high error rates.

children.title: Prometheus: Scraping & PromQL
children.description: Prometheus pulls metrics from instrumented endpoints on a fixed interval and stores them as time-series data. Writing PromQL queries incorrectly — especially around rate vs irate vs increase — leads to dashboards that misrepresent error rates and trigger false alerts during deployments.

children.title: Grafana Dashboards & Alerting
children.description: Grafana visualizes Prometheus data and routes alert notifications to Slack, PagerDuty, or email. A dashboard that only shows average latency hides the 99th-percentile spikes that users actually experience, creating a false sense of health.

children.title: Loki & Promtail: Log Aggregation
children.description: Loki indexes only log metadata (labels) rather than full text, making it vastly cheaper to operate than Elasticsearch for most use cases. Shipping logs without structured labels makes it impossible to filter by service, namespace, or pod during a production incident.

children.title: Structured Logging Practices
children.description: Structured logs (JSON with consistent fields like request_id, user_id, and duration_ms) are machine-parseable and directly queryable in Loki or CloudWatch. Free-text logs require regex parsing that breaks on the first format change and makes cross-service correlation effectively impossible.

children.title: OpenTelemetry Instrumentation
children.description: OpenTelemetry provides a vendor-neutral SDK for emitting traces, metrics, and logs from any service. Without distributed tracing, a slow API response that involves five microservices is a black box — you can see the symptom but not the cause.

children.title: AWS X-Ray Tracing
children.description: X-Ray automatically traces requests through Lambda, API Gateway, and ECS, correlating them into service maps without requiring custom instrumentation. Missing trace propagation headers at any service boundary breaks the chain and leaves a gap that looks like an instant hop but hides real latency.

children.title: Defining SLIs, SLOs & Error Budgets
children.description: SLOs translate reliability aspirations into concrete, measurable targets that balance feature velocity against stability. A team without an error budget has no principled way to decide whether to ship a risky feature or spend the sprint on reliability work.

children.title: CloudWatch Container Insights
children.description: Container Insights automatically collects container-level CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics from EKS and ECS without manual Prometheus setup. Missing resource utilization visibility means you size node groups by gut feeling, consistently leading to either over-spending or node pressure evictions.

cloud.title: Security & Cost Optimization
cloud.description: Security and cost are the two areas that separate good cloud engineers from great ones. Apply defense-in-depth and understand the cloud billing model to avoid expensive surprises.
topics: IAM — least privilege, permission boundaries, service accounts
HashiCorp Vault — secrets engine, dynamic credentials
Network security — private subnets, VPN, WAF
Image scanning — Trivy, ECR native scanning
AWS Cost Explorer — reserved instances, savings plans
cloud.challenge: Audit your AWS setup against the Well-Architected Framework — fix IAM over-permissions, add Vault for DB credentials, set up billing alerts, and migrate non-critical workloads to Spot.

children.title: IAM Least Privilege & Permission Boundaries
children.description: Permission boundaries are an IAM guardrail that caps the maximum permissions any role can grant, even if a developer accidentally attaches an overly broad policy. Without them, a single misconfigured role in a multi-account organization can escalate to full administrator access.

children.title: HashiCorp Vault: Dynamic Credentials
children.description: Vault generates short-lived, auto-expiring database credentials on demand, eliminating the long-lived passwords that are shared across teams and never rotated. A static DB password that exists for months is far more likely to appear in a breach dataset than one that expires after an hour.

children.title: Private Subnets, NAT & WAF
children.description: Databases and internal services should live in private subnets accessible only through a NAT gateway or VPN. A WAF in front of your public endpoints blocks common web exploits (SQL injection, XSS) before they reach application code, which matters especially for teams without dedicated AppSec engineers.

children.title: Container Image Scanning with Trivy
children.description: Trivy scans container images, filesystems, and IaC configurations for known CVEs before they are deployed. A base image pulled six months ago and never rescanned typically contains dozens of published vulnerabilities that have since been fixed in newer releases.

children.title: AWS Cost Explorer & Savings Plans
children.description: Cost Explorer surfaces per-service spending trends, and Savings Plans offer up to 66% discounts on predictable compute in exchange for a 1 or 3-year commitment. Teams that never review their Cost Explorer dashboard regularly discover they are paying full on-demand prices for workloads that have run continuously for years.

children.title: Spot & Preemptible Instances
children.description: Spot instances offer up to 90% cost reduction but can be reclaimed with 2-minute notice. Stateless, fault-tolerant workloads like CI runners, batch jobs, and worker pools are perfect Spot candidates, while stateful databases are not — confusing the two causes production data loss.

children.title: AWS Well-Architected Framework
children.description: The Well-Architected Framework provides a structured lens across five pillars — operational excellence, security, reliability, performance, and cost — for evaluating any architecture. Skipping this review is how teams ship architectures with obvious single points of failure that survive untouched for years.

children.title: AWS CloudTrail & Config
children.description: CloudTrail records every API call made in your account with the caller identity, timestamp, and source IP. Without it, a security incident investigation cannot answer the most basic question: who changed what and when.

cloud.title: Multi-Region Architecture and Disaster Recovery
cloud.description: Design globally distributed systems on AWS that survive regional failures using active-active and active-passive topologies backed by automated failover. Apply Terraform to codify multi-region infrastructure and implement RTO/RPO targets through tested DR runbooks.
topics: Route 53 latency and failover routing policies
Aurora Global Database and cross-region read replicas
S3 Cross-Region Replication and object consistency
Global Accelerator for anycast traffic routing
Chaos and DR drills with AWS Fault Injection Simulator
RTO and RPO target definition and measurement

resources.label: Cloud Architecture Patterns by Bill Wilder

cloud.challenge: Deploy a stateless API behind Global Accelerator across two AWS regions with Aurora Global Database, automate regional failover with Route 53 health checks, and run a simulated regional outage to validate your RTO target.

children.title: Active-Active vs Active-Passive Topologies
children.description: Active-active serves live traffic from multiple regions simultaneously for maximum resilience; active-passive keeps a warm standby ready to receive traffic after failover. Choosing the wrong topology results in either paying for capacity that never serves real users or failing to meet an RTO target during an actual regional outage.

children.title: Route 53 Latency & Failover Routing
children.description: Route 53 health checks drive automated DNS failover between regions without human intervention. A health check threshold that is too sensitive triggers unnecessary failovers during transient spikes; one that is too lenient lets an entire region stay in rotation after it has stopped serving traffic correctly.

children.title: Aurora Global Database
children.description: Aurora Global Database replicates data across regions with typical lag under one second, enabling low-latency reads globally and fast failover. Using standard RDS cross-region replicas instead means minutes of lag and a manual, error-prone promotion process during a real incident.

children.title: S3 Cross-Region Replication
children.description: S3 Cross-Region Replication automatically copies objects to a destination bucket in another region, providing geographic redundancy for backups and static assets. Without it, an S3 bucket in a degraded region takes all dependent assets offline and breaks deployments that pull configuration from that bucket.

children.title: AWS Global Accelerator
children.description: Global Accelerator routes user traffic over the AWS backbone network rather than the public internet, reducing latency and providing static anycast IPs for multi-region endpoints. Without it, traffic to a degraded region keeps routing over the public internet until DNS TTLs expire.

children.title: CloudFront for Global CDN
children.description: CloudFront caches content at 450+ edge locations to serve static and dynamic content with low latency worldwide. Serving assets directly from an origin S3 bucket or EC2 instance introduces cross-region latency on every request and massively increases data transfer costs.

children.title: RTO & RPO Definition and Measurement
children.description: RTO (Recovery Time Objective) defines how long you can be down; RPO (Recovery Point Objective) defines how much data you can lose. Defining these targets after an incident rather than before means your DR architecture was never validated against real business requirements.

children.title: AWS Fault Injection Simulator
children.description: FIS lets you run controlled chaos experiments — terminating instances, injecting latency, or blocking network traffic — to validate that your failover mechanisms actually work before a real failure exposes them. DR runbooks that have never been executed under simulated failure conditions are fiction, not engineering.

cloud.title: FinOps, Platform Engineering and SRE Practices
cloud.description: Shift from infrastructure provisioning to building internal developer platforms that give teams self-service access to golden-path templates, cost visibility, and reliability tooling. Apply SRE principles including toil reduction, capacity planning, and error budget policies.
topics: FinOps framework and cloud cost allocation tagging strategies
AWS Cost Explorer, Budgets, and Savings Plans optimization
Internal developer platforms and Backstage scaffolding
Golden-path templates and platform-team operating models
Toil measurement and automation priority frameworks
Error budget policies and SRE team topologies
cloud.challenge: Set up a Backstage instance with one software template that provisions a Terraform-managed S3-backed microservice, tag all resources for cost allocation, and create a monthly budget alert tied to the project team.

children.title: Cloud Cost Allocation & Tagging
children.description: Consistent resource tagging (environment, team, product, cost-center) is the foundation of FinOps — without it you cannot attribute cloud costs to business units or enforce chargeback. A single untagged resource type can make entire service cost lines invisible, undermining every budget conversation.

children.title: AWS Budgets & Cost Anomaly Detection
children.description: AWS Budgets sends proactive alerts when spending exceeds defined thresholds; Cost Anomaly Detection uses ML to flag unexpected cost spikes automatically. Without these guardrails, a forgotten NAT gateway or runaway data transfer can silently add thousands of dollars before anyone notices.

children.title: Backstage Software Catalog
children.description: Backstage provides a centralized developer portal where teams can discover services, see ownership, and access golden-path templates. Without a software catalog, onboarding a new engineer means a treasure hunt through five Confluence pages, three Slack channels, and tribal knowledge that no longer matches reality.

children.title: Golden-Path Templates & Scaffolding
children.description: Golden-path templates pre-wire security, observability, CI/CD, and cost tagging into every new service from day one. Without them, each team reinvents infrastructure plumbing differently, creating a sprawl of snowflake services with inconsistent security postures.

children.title: Toil Measurement & Automation
children.description: Toil is manual, repetitive, automatable work that scales with traffic rather than with engineering headcount. Teams that do not measure and reduce toil eventually spend more time on operational chores than on reliability improvements, inverting the purpose of an SRE function.

children.title: Error Budget Policies
children.description: An error budget policy defines what happens when you exhaust your reliability budget — typically a feature freeze until reliability work restores the budget. Without a written policy, the error budget remains a metric rather than a governance mechanism, and reliability work is perpetually deprioritized.

children.title: Predictive Autoscaling
children.description: Predictive autoscaling uses historical load patterns to provision capacity before demand arrives, rather than reacting after latency has already spiked. Purely reactive scaling always lags the traffic curve — new instances take minutes to become healthy while users experience the degradation in real time.

children.title: Capacity Planning from Metrics
children.description: Capacity planning requires trending resource utilization over weeks to identify saturation points before they affect availability. Flying blind without utilization trends leads to surprise node pressure evictions at peak traffic and over-provisioned clusters wasting budget during off-peak hours.

cicd.title: CI/CD Foundations
cicd.description: This layer introduces the core ideas behind continuous integration and continuous delivery. It matters because every modern delivery pipeline builds on these foundational concepts and version control habits.
topics: What CI and CD mean and how they differ

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana CI/CD Crash Course

cicd.challenge: Create a repository with a single GitHub Actions workflow that runs a linter on every push.

children.title: Continuous Integration vs Continuous Delivery
children.description: CI is the practice of merging code frequently and validating it automatically; CD extends that automation all the way to deployment. Conflating the two leads to teams that build automatically but still deploy manually, defeating the speed and safety benefits of the whole model.

children.title: Pipeline Triggers and Fast Feedback
children.description: Pipelines should fire on every push, pull request, and tag so developers get results within minutes of a change. Without tight feedback loops, broken code accumulates and the cost of fixing it grows exponentially with every hour of delay.

resources.label: GitHub Actions Events that Trigger Workflows

children.title: Trunk-Based vs Feature Branch Development
children.description: Trunk-based development keeps integration continuous by committing to main frequently, while long-lived feature branches delay feedback and cause merge conflicts. Understanding both models lets you choose the right strategy for your team's risk tolerance and release cadence.

children.title: YAML Workflow Syntax
children.description: GitHub Actions pipelines are declared entirely in YAML, making syntax errors a silent killer of automation. Mastering workflow structure — jobs, steps, expressions, and context variables — is a prerequisite for writing reliable pipelines rather than pipelines that look correct but silently skip steps.

children.title: Idempotent Builds
children.description: A build that produces different outputs on successive runs from the same source is unreliable and breaks reproducibility. Pinning dependency versions, locking tool versions, and avoiding ambient state are the habits that make builds idempotent and trustworthy.

children.title: Shift-Left Quality
children.description: Running linters, type checkers, and unit tests as the very first pipeline stage catches defects at the cheapest possible moment. Teams that push quality checks to the end of the pipeline pay the highest cost per bug because downstream stages amplify the disruption of a late failure.

children.title: Pipeline as an Afterthought
children.description: Teams that add CI/CD after the codebase matures inherit untestable code, manual steps baked into developer muscle memory, and no culture of treating a broken build as a blocker. Introducing pipelines at project start forces testability and discipline from day one.

children.title: Ignoring Flaky Tests
children.description: A test suite with even a small percentage of flaky tests erodes trust in the entire pipeline; developers begin skipping failures and re-running jobs until they pass, which is the beginning of broken CI culture. Tracking and fixing flaky tests is not optional — it is a prerequisite for fast, reliable delivery.

resources.label: GitHub Actions Re-running Workflows and Jobs

cicd.title: Build and Test Automation
cicd.description: This layer focuses on automating builds and running test suites inside pipelines. It matters because reliable automated testing is what makes continuous integration trustworthy.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitHub Actions Tutorial

cicd.challenge: Extend the workflow to build a Docker image and run a test matrix across multiple language versions.

children.title: Matrix Builds and Parallel Jobs
children.description: Matrix strategies let a single workflow definition run tests across multiple language versions, operating systems, or dependency combinations simultaneously. Without matrices, teams either under-test or serially execute combinations that could complete in parallel, making pipelines unnecessarily slow.

children.title: Dependency Caching for Speed
children.description: Restoring node_modules, Maven repositories, or pip caches from a previous run can cut build times from minutes to seconds. Misconfigured cache keys cause stale dependencies that mask real failures, making cache key design a correctness concern as much as a performance one.

children.title: Unit vs Integration Tests in CI
children.description: Unit tests should run first because they are fast and isolate failures; integration tests run services together and are slower, so running them without isolation causes environmental flakiness. Separating them into distinct pipeline stages gives clear signal about whether the failure is logic or wiring.

children.title: Test Coverage Reporting
children.description: Publishing coverage reports as pipeline artifacts gives teams visibility into which code paths are tested and surfaces regressions in coverage before merge. Coverage without a baseline threshold enforced in the pipeline is decorative — it informs but does not protect.

children.title: Building Container Images in CI
children.description: Building Docker images inside a CI runner exposes the runner to daemon socket privileges which is a significant security boundary; using rootless builds or dedicated build services like Kaniko avoids this risk. Misunderstanding this leads to pipelines that work locally but fail in hardened environments or expose host access to untrusted code.

resources.label: GitHub Actions Build and Push Docker Images

children.title: Multi-Stage Dockerfiles
children.description: Multi-stage builds separate build tooling from the final runtime image, dramatically reducing image size and attack surface. Teams that skip multi-stage builds ship compilers, test frameworks, and development secrets inside production images, which is both wasteful and a security risk.

children.title: Fail-Fast Job Configuration
children.description: Pipelines that continue executing downstream jobs after a critical failure waste runner minutes and delay the developer's feedback. Setting fail-fast behavior and adding explicit dependency guards between jobs ensures engineers see the root cause immediately without sifting through cascading noise.

resources.label: GitHub Actions Job Needs and Outputs

children.title: Build Notifications
children.description: A pipeline that fails silently is equivalent to no pipeline at all; teams must configure Slack, email, or GitHub status checks so failures are impossible to miss. Notification fatigue from noisy pipelines is equally harmful and trains teams to ignore alerts, so targeted notifications on real failures are the goal.

cicd.title: Pipeline as Code with Jenkins
cicd.description: This layer covers declarative and scripted pipelines using Jenkins. It matters because many enterprises run Jenkins and pipeline as code keeps delivery reproducible.
cicd.challenge: Recreate your build and test pipeline as a Jenkinsfile running on a containerized agent.

children.title: Controller and Agent Model
children.description: Jenkins separates orchestration on the controller from execution on agents, enabling parallel workloads across many machines or containers. Running builds directly on the controller is an anti-pattern that ties pipeline execution to the availability and security boundary of the central server.

children.title: Declarative vs Scripted Pipelines
children.description: Declarative pipelines impose a structured syntax that is easier to read and validate, while scripted pipelines offer full Groovy flexibility at the cost of complexity and hidden footguns. Teams that default to scripted pipelines for simple cases accumulate pipeline code that only one person understands.

children.title: Stages and Post Actions
children.description: Stages make pipeline progress visible in the Jenkins UI and allow fine-grained success or failure reporting per logical phase. Post actions — always, success, failure, cleanup — ensure teardown and notifications happen regardless of build outcome, preventing resource leaks and silent failures.

children.title: Shared Libraries
children.description: Jenkins shared libraries let teams extract common pipeline logic — Docker build helpers, notification functions, test reporters — into a versioned Groovy library consumed by all Jenkinsfiles. Without shared libraries, the same boilerplate is copied across dozens of repos and diverges silently over time.

children.title: Credentials and Secrets Handling
children.description: Jenkins has a built-in credentials store that injects secrets as environment variables or files into build steps without echoing them to logs. Hardcoding credentials in a Jenkinsfile or using environment variables declared in plain text is a critical security failure that is difficult to audit and impossible to rotate.

children.title: Agent Security and Isolation
children.description: Each build on a shared agent inherits files, environment variables, and cached credentials from previous builds unless the workspace is cleaned explicitly. Using containerized or ephemeral agents guarantees build isolation and prevents credential leakage between unrelated pipelines.

children.title: Plugin Management and Version Control
children.description: Jenkins functionality lives in plugins that must be version-pinned and tested before upgrading in production; an untested plugin update can silently break all pipelines. Managing plugins as code with the Jenkins Configuration as Code plugin prevents configuration drift between controller instances.

children.title: Pipeline Durability Settings
children.description: Jenkins persists pipeline state to disk so builds can survive controller restarts, but this comes with performance cost; tuning durability settings per pipeline prevents performance degradation on high-throughput pipelines. Ignoring durability settings leads to either data loss or unnecessary I/O that slows the entire system.

cicd.title: Artifact Management and Versioning
cicd.description: This layer teaches how to publish, version, and store build outputs. It matters because traceable artifacts are essential for reliable releases and rollbacks.
cicd.challenge: Add a job that builds, versions with semantic tags, and pushes the image to a registry on merges to main.

children.title: Semantic Versioning
children.description: Semantic versioning encodes breaking changes, new features, and patches into a three-part version number that consumers can interpret without reading the changelog. Ignoring semver leads to consumers who cannot safely automate dependency updates and teams who ship breaking changes in patch releases.

children.title: Immutable Artifacts and Git Tagging
children.description: An artifact tagged with a Git SHA or semver tag is immutable — the same tag always refers to the same binary, making deployments reproducible and rollbacks reliable. Mutable tags like latest cause pipelines and deployments to silently pull different code on different runs, which is one of the most common sources of environment inconsistency.

children.title: Container Registry Push in CI
children.description: Pushing a container image to a registry as a pipeline step creates the single source of truth for all downstream deployments; anything not in the registry was never validated by CI. Teams that build images locally and push manually bypass CI entirely, shipping untested code to production.

children.title: Retention and Cleanup Policies
children.description: Registries without retention policies accumulate thousands of images that cost money and make it impossible to identify which tags are safe to delete. Automating retention — keeping the last N tags and all tagged semver releases — keeps storage costs predictable and registries navigable.

children.title: Automated Release Notes
children.description: Generating release notes from commit messages or pull request titles ensures every release has a human-readable changelog without manual effort. Teams without automated release notes either skip changelogs entirely or maintain them by hand, which diverges from reality within weeks.

children.title: Artifact Provenance and SBOM
children.description: Provenance metadata cryptographically links an artifact to the source commit, build environment, and signing key, enabling consumers to verify they received exactly what CI produced. Ignoring provenance exposes the supply chain to substitution attacks where a tampered artifact replaces the genuine build output.

children.title: Image Signing
children.description: Signing container images with Cosign or Notary ensures that only images produced by trusted CI pipelines can be deployed; unsigned images rejected at the admission controller level prevent supply chain attacks from reaching production. Without signing, there is no way to distinguish a legitimate CI build from a tampered or hand-built image.

children.title: Vulnerability Scanning Before Push
children.description: Running Trivy or Grype against an image before pushing to the registry gates the artifact on a known-clean bill of health at build time. Scanning only in production or after deployment is too late — the vulnerability is already in the registry and potentially running in pre-production environments.

cicd.title: Deployment Strategies
cicd.description: This layer explores how to ship code safely to environments. It matters because the deployment strategy directly controls user-facing risk during releases.
cicd.challenge: Implement a canary deployment job that promotes to production only after a manual approval gate.

children.title: Blue-Green Deployments
children.description: Blue-green deployments maintain two identical environments so traffic can be switched instantly, making rollbacks a router change rather than a code revert. Teams that attempt to roll back by redeploying old code under load discover that the redeployment itself takes minutes and causes a second outage.

children.title: Canary Releases
children.description: Canary releases route a small percentage of traffic to the new version, exposing real user behavior while limiting blast radius; success metrics gate promotion to 100% of traffic. Deploying all-or-nothing to production without a canary phase means a bug in a new release instantly affects every user.

children.title: Promotion Gates and Manual Approvals
children.description: Requiring a human approval before promoting from staging to production forces a deliberate decision and creates an audit trail; automated promotion without gates ships every change to production including regressions that passed automated checks. GitHub Actions environments let you configure required reviewers as a native gate.

children.title: Environment Parity
children.description: Staging environments that differ from production in infrastructure, data, or configuration produce false confidence — bugs that only appear under production conditions slip through. Maintaining parity through identical Terraform modules, production-like datasets, and the same secrets management path is what makes staging a reliable gate.

children.title: Automated Rollback Triggers
children.description: Linking deployment health checks to automated rollback — rolling back when error rate exceeds a threshold within the first five minutes — converts a manual incident response into a self-healing system. Without automated rollback, every failed deployment requires an on-call engineer to manually intervene, adding minutes of downtime.

children.title: Rolling Updates and Zero Downtime
children.description: Rolling updates replace pods or instances gradually so at least one healthy instance is always serving traffic; misconfiguring max-unavailable or readiness probes causes rolling updates to take the service completely offline before new instances are ready. Kubernetes rolling update parameters must match the application's startup time.

children.title: CircleCI Workflows and Orbs
children.description: CircleCI orbs are reusable pipeline packages that encapsulate common deployment patterns — Docker push, Kubernetes deploy, Slack notify — without copy-pasting YAML across projects. Teams that write inline pipeline logic instead of using orbs accumulate hundreds of lines of duplicated configuration that diverges silently.

children.title: Feature Flags for Safe Releases
children.description: Feature flags decouple deployment from release so new code ships dark and is enabled for specific users or percentages, reducing deployment risk to near zero. Without feature flags, every deployment is also a release, meaning rollback is the only option when new behavior causes problems.

cicd.title: GitOps with ArgoCD
cicd.description: This layer introduces declarative continuous delivery driven by Git as the source of truth. It matters because GitOps makes deployments auditable, reversible, and self-healing.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitOps with ArgoCD

cicd.challenge: Set up ArgoCD to continuously sync your application from a Git repository to a cluster.

children.title: Git as the Single Source of Truth
children.description: In GitOps, the desired state of every environment is declared in Git; any change to the cluster that is not reflected in Git is drift and will be automatically corrected. Teams that allow kubectl apply without a corresponding Git commit lose auditability and find themselves unable to reconstruct what is actually running.

children.title: Pull vs Push Deployment Models
children.description: GitOps uses a pull model — an in-cluster agent polls Git and applies changes — rather than a CI pipeline pushing to the cluster; this eliminates the need to expose cluster credentials to external systems and makes the cluster self-healing. Push-based CD requires outbound firewall rules, credential rotation, and offers no automatic drift correction.

children.title: Application Sync and Health Checks
children.description: ArgoCD evaluates application health by comparing live cluster state to the desired state in Git and reports sync status; an application can be synced but unhealthy if pods are crash-looping. Relying solely on sync status without monitoring health status produces false confidence that a deployment succeeded.

resources.label: Argo CD Sync and Diff Documentation

children.title: Sync Waves and Resource Hooks
children.description: Sync waves define the order in which resources are applied during a sync — ensuring CRDs exist before the resources that use them and database migrations run before the application starts. Ignoring sync ordering causes deployment failures that are hard to reproduce and require manual cleanup of partially applied state.

children.title: Automated Drift Correction
children.description: ArgoCD's auto-sync feature corrects drift immediately when cluster state diverges from Git, ensuring the declared state is always what runs; without auto-sync, drift accumulates silently until someone notices or a deployment overwrites it with something else. Choosing between auto-sync and manual sync is a governance decision about how much human oversight is required per environment.

children.title: Rollbacks Through Git History
children.description: In GitOps, rolling back a deployment means reverting a Git commit, which is auditable, testable, and reviewable through the normal pull request process. Imperative rollback commands applied directly to the cluster bypass Git and create drift, defeating the entire model.

children.title: App of Apps Pattern
children.description: The app of apps pattern uses a parent ArgoCD application that manages child applications, enabling a single Git repository to declare all clusters and environments; without it, onboarding a new environment requires manually creating dozens of ArgoCD resources instead of one Git commit. This pattern is the foundation of scalable GitOps platform engineering.

resources.label: Argo CD App of Apps Pattern

children.title: Image Updater Automation
children.description: Argo CD Image Updater polls a container registry and writes updated image tags back to Git, closing the loop between a CI push and a GitOps deployment without manual PR editing. Without this automation, teams must write custom scripts or manually update image tags in Git every time CI produces a new artifact.

cicd.title: Secrets and Security in Pipelines
cicd.description: This layer covers securely handling credentials and signing artifacts in delivery pipelines. It matters because pipelines are a prime target for supply chain attacks.
cicd.challenge: Replace static cloud credentials with OIDC federation and add an image scan gate to the pipeline.

children.title: Never Store Secrets in Git
children.description: Secrets committed to Git — even in private repositories and even if deleted — are permanently exposed because Git history is immutable and repository credentials are frequently over-shared. The only safe position is that secrets never enter Git, enforced by pre-commit hooks and secret scanning tools.

children.title: OIDC Federation for Cloud Access
children.description: OIDC federation lets GitHub Actions authenticate to AWS, GCP, or Azure using short-lived tokens issued by the identity provider, eliminating the need for long-lived cloud credentials stored as repository secrets. Static credentials that never expire are one of the most common vectors for supply chain compromise.

children.title: HashiCorp Vault in Pipelines
children.description: Vault issues dynamic, short-lived credentials on demand — a database password valid for five minutes — so pipelines never hold a credential long enough for it to be exploited after a breach. Static secrets injected from a secrets manager at deploy time are better than Git, but still create a window of exposure that Vault's dynamic secrets model eliminates.

children.title: Sealed Secrets for GitOps
children.description: Bitnami Sealed Secrets encrypts a Kubernetes Secret into a SealedSecret that can be safely committed to Git and decrypted only by the in-cluster controller, bridging the gap between GitOps's requirement to store everything in Git and the requirement to never store plaintext secrets there.

children.title: Least Privilege for Runner Permissions
children.description: GitHub Actions GITHUB_TOKEN permissions default to read-write for the entire repository; scoping them to the minimum required per job prevents a compromised step from pushing malicious code or creating releases. The principle of least privilege applied to pipeline permissions is one of the highest-leverage security improvements available.

children.title: Dependency and Image Scanning
children.description: Running Dependabot, Snyk, or Trivy in the pipeline gates merges on known-vulnerable dependencies and base images, preventing vulnerabilities from reaching the registry. Scanning only at the registry level is a lagging indicator — the vulnerability has already been built, tested, and pushed before anyone is notified.

children.title: SLSA Framework Compliance
children.description: SLSA (Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts) defines a four-level framework for hardening the build and publish process against tampering; achieving SLSA Level 2 requires provenance generation and a hosted build service, which GitHub Actions satisfies out of the box with attestations. Ignoring SLSA leaves artifact integrity entirely on trust rather than cryptographic verification.

children.title: Auditing Pipeline Access
children.description: Every pipeline run that accesses a secret, deploys to an environment, or calls an external API should produce an audit log entry; GitHub's audit log API and Vault's audit device provide this visibility and are essential for post-incident forensics. Without audit logs, a supply chain compromise may go undetected for months.

cicd.title: Pipeline Observability and Metrics
cicd.description: This layer teaches how to measure and visualize pipeline health and delivery performance. It matters because you cannot improve delivery speed without measuring it.
cicd.challenge: Export pipeline run metrics and build a Grafana dashboard showing deployment frequency and lead time.

children.title: Deployment Frequency and Lead Time
children.description: Deployment frequency measures how often code reaches production and lead time measures how long it takes from commit to deployment; together they quantify the throughput of the delivery system. Teams that do not measure these metrics cannot demonstrate improvement and cannot identify whether a new pipeline change made delivery faster or slower.

children.title: Change Failure Rate and Recovery Time
children.description: Change failure rate measures the percentage of deployments that cause an incident; mean time to restore measures how long recovery takes when something goes wrong. High deployment frequency with a high change failure rate is not elite performance — it is a sign that speed has been prioritized at the expense of stability.

children.title: Pipeline Duration Tracking
children.description: Tracking pipeline duration over time surfaces regressions — a pipeline that grew from 3 minutes to 15 minutes signals test suite bloat, cache misses, or added stages that need to be parallelized. Without tracking, teams only notice pipeline slowness when it becomes painful enough to complain about.

children.title: Failure Rate and Flaky Job Detection
children.description: Tracking which jobs fail most often and which fail intermittently exposes systemic unreliability that aggregate pass rates hide; a job with a 20% flakiness rate destroys team confidence even if 80% of runs succeed. Exporting pipeline metadata to a time series database makes these patterns visible and actionable.

children.title: Grafana Dashboards for Delivery
children.description: A Grafana dashboard showing deployment frequency, pipeline duration, and failure rate gives engineering leadership real-time visibility into delivery health without polling developers. Dashboards that live in presentations rather than production monitoring are consulted during planning and ignored during incidents.

children.title: Notifications and Escalation
children.description: Pipeline failure notifications must reach the right person immediately — the author who broke the build — not a shared channel where everyone assumes someone else will investigate. Configuring author-targeted notifications from GitHub Actions or Slack webhooks closes the feedback loop and reduces mean time to fix.

children.title: Bottleneck Analysis
children.description: Pipeline bottlenecks — the slowest stage that determines total duration — must be identified before optimization efforts can have impact; parallelizing a fast stage while ignoring the slow one saves nothing. Critical path analysis of job dependency graphs reveals where investment in caching or parallelism returns the most value.

children.title: Blameless Pipeline Postmortems
children.description: When a pipeline failure causes a production incident, a blameless postmortem focused on system improvement — rather than individual blame — produces action items that prevent recurrence and builds psychological safety for reporting future issues. Teams without this culture hide incidents, which compounds the underlying reliability debt.

cicd.title: Scaling and Reusable Pipelines
cicd.description: This layer focuses on sharing and standardizing pipelines across many repositories. It matters because consistency at scale reduces maintenance and enforces best practices.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana CI/CD at Scale

cicd.challenge: Refactor your pipeline into a reusable workflow consumed by two separate service repositories.

children.title: GitHub Actions Reusable Workflows
children.description: Reusable workflows let teams define a pipeline once in a central repository and call it from any other workflow, ensuring every service uses the same security-hardened, tested pipeline without duplication. Copy-pasted workflows diverge immediately and silently — security patches applied to one copy never reach the others.

children.title: Monorepo Pipeline Strategies
children.description: Monorepos require pipelines that detect which packages changed and run only the relevant build and test jobs; running the full test suite for every commit in a large monorepo makes pipelines too slow to be useful. Path filters and dependency graphs in GitHub Actions matrix builds solve this by scoping work to affected services.

children.title: Ephemeral Runners and Auto-Scaling
children.description: Persistent self-hosted runners accumulate state between builds and become a source of non-reproducibility; ephemeral runners — created per job and destroyed after — provide clean isolation and scale to zero cost when idle. Runner auto-scaling on Kubernetes using Actions Runner Controller is the production pattern for cost-efficient scale.

children.title: Concurrency Control
children.description: Unlimited pipeline concurrency causes resource contention on shared infrastructure and can result in dozens of simultaneous deployments to the same environment; concurrency groups in GitHub Actions prevent this by canceling or queuing subsequent runs. Without concurrency control, two simultaneous deploys to production can race and leave the cluster in an undefined state.

children.title: Required Status Checks and Branch Protection
children.description: Branch protection rules that require specific pipeline status checks to pass before merge enforce quality gates at the repository level, preventing developers or automation from bypassing CI. Without required status checks, broken pipelines are dismissed as noise and code merges proceed anyway.

children.title: Templating and Standardization
children.description: Providing golden-path pipeline templates that teams adopt at project creation ensures every service starts with security scanning, proper caching, and deployment gates already configured. Platform teams that do not provide templates find themselves maintaining hundreds of slightly different pipelines that each require individual attention.

children.title: Pipeline Minute Optimization
children.description: GitHub Actions bills by the minute for hosted runners; a test suite that runs in 20 minutes because of sequential jobs costs four times more than the same suite parallelized to 5 minutes. Investing in pipeline performance analysis pays for itself quickly at scale and reduces developer waiting time simultaneously.

children.title: Cache Hit Rate Monitoring
children.description: A cache hit rate below 80% on dependency caches indicates that cache keys are too specific or that dependencies are being modified unexpectedly, both of which waste pipeline time on repeated downloads. Monitoring cache effectiveness alongside build duration gives a complete picture of pipeline efficiency.

cicd.title: Production Delivery Operations
cicd.description: This layer covers operating delivery systems reliably in production at organizational scale. It matters because a delivery platform must stay available, secure, and fast under real load.
cicd.challenge: Operate an end-to-end GitOps delivery platform with progressive rollouts and automated rollback on failure.

children.title: Multi-Environment Release Governance
children.description: Release governance defines who can approve a promotion to production, under what conditions, and what evidence is required — test results, security scan passes, stakeholder sign-off. Without governance, production deployments happen whenever a pipeline succeeds, removing the human judgment required to manage business risk.

children.title: Compliance and Audit Trails
children.description: Regulated industries require evidence that every production deployment was tested, approved by an authorized person, and deployed from a known artifact; GitHub's deployment API and environment protection rules produce this audit trail automatically. Teams that cannot produce deployment evidence on demand fail SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audits regardless of their actual security practices.

children.title: Argo Rollouts for Progressive Delivery
children.description: Argo Rollouts extends Kubernetes Deployments with canary and blue-green strategies driven by analysis templates that query metrics sources — Prometheus, Datadog, New Relic — and automatically promote or abort based on real traffic data. Manual canary management without Argo Rollouts requires a human to watch dashboards and manually adjust traffic splits, which does not scale.

children.title: Automated Rollback on Failure
children.description: Linking Argo Rollouts analysis to error rate or latency SLOs means a failed canary is automatically rolled back without paging anyone; this converts a potential incident into a non-event. Without automated rollback, every failed canary requires a human to notice the degradation, make a decision, and execute the rollback — during which time users are impacted.

children.title: Failed Deploy Incident Response
children.description: A deployment that causes a production incident must trigger a defined response — automated rollback, incident channel creation, on-call notification — rather than relying on someone to notice the deployment and dashboards simultaneously. Runbooks for failed deploys must be tested in drills before they are needed at 3am.

children.title: Pipeline Disaster Recovery
children.description: The delivery platform itself is critical infrastructure; if GitHub Actions or ArgoCD is unavailable, teams need a documented manual fallback procedure to deploy from known-good artifacts without the automation. A delivery system with no DR plan means a platform outage becomes a deployment freeze that can last hours.

children.title: Delivery Review Cadence
children.description: Regular delivery reviews that examine DORA metrics trends, pipeline reliability, and deployment risk create accountability for improving the delivery system as a product rather than an afterthought. Teams without a delivery review cadence drift toward treating slow, unreliable pipelines as a fact of life rather than an engineering problem to solve.

resources.label: Accelerate — Building and Scaling High Performing Technology Organizations

children.title: Platform Team and Golden Paths
children.description: A platform team that owns the delivery infrastructure and provides golden-path templates relieves product teams of pipeline maintenance burden and ensures security and compliance requirements are met automatically. Without a platform team, every service team independently reinvents pipeline patterns at different quality levels.

sre.title: SRE Fundamentals
sre.description: This layer introduces the principles and mindset of site reliability engineering. It matters because reliability work is grounded in shared definitions of risk, toil, and service objectives.
topics: What SRE is and how it relates to DevOps
sre.challenge: Write a one-page reliability charter defining SLIs and an error budget for a sample service.

children.title: What SRE Actually Is
children.description: SRE is an engineering discipline that applies software thinking to operations problems. Without understanding this distinction, teams treat SREs as glorified sysadmins and fail to leverage the automation leverage that makes the role valuable.

resources.label: SRE vs DevOps — Google Cloud Blog

children.title: Reliability vs Feature Velocity
children.description: The tension between shipping features fast and maintaining reliability is the central trade-off in SRE. Misunderstanding this leads to either a reliability-obsessed culture that ships nothing or a move-fast culture that burns out on-call engineers with constant incidents.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — Motivation and Terms

children.title: Error Budget as a Policy Tool
children.description: An error budget converts an abstract SLO target into a concrete spending account teams can deplete by shipping risky changes. Without an error budget policy, SLOs are just aspirational numbers that no one acts on.

resources.label: Google SRE Book — Motivation for Error Budgets

children.title: Blameless Culture
children.description: Blameless postmortems assume that engineers make the best decisions with the information available at the time. Without psychological safety, teams hide near-misses and system fragility grows silently until catastrophic failure.

children.title: Identifying Toil
children.description: Toil is manual, repetitive work that scales with service growth and provides no lasting value. Failing to identify and measure toil means engineering time drains into operations instead of building the automation that prevents future incidents.

children.title: Automation Priority Frameworks
children.description: Not all toil is worth automating — the cost of building and maintaining automation must be weighed against the toil it eliminates. Without a prioritization framework, teams automate the interesting problems rather than the highest-impact ones.

children.title: Choosing Meaningful SLIs
children.description: An SLI is a quantitative measure of service behavior from the user's perspective. Picking the wrong SLI — measuring what is easy to instrument rather than what users care about — produces SLOs that pass even during bad user experiences.

children.title: SLAs as Business Contracts
children.description: An SLA is the external agreement with consequences for missing reliability targets, while an SLO is the internal engineering target set tighter to provide a buffer. Confusing the two leads to setting external commitments the engineering team cannot actually meet.

resources.label: Google SRE Book — Service Level Objectives
PagerDuty — SLA vs SLO vs SLI

sre.title: Metrics and Monitoring with Prometheus
sre.description: This layer teaches how to collect and query time series metrics. It matters because metrics are the foundation of understanding system behavior and health.
sre.challenge: Instrument a Go service with custom metrics and scrape them with a local Prometheus instance.

children.title: Pull Model and Service Discovery
children.description: Prometheus scrapes targets on a schedule rather than waiting for them to push data, which means it controls the timing and can detect when a target disappears. Misunderstanding pull semantics leads to architects grafting push pipelines onto Prometheus and introducing reliability gaps.

children.title: Metric Types
children.description: Counters, gauges, histograms, and summaries each encode different semantics and must be used for the right kind of measurement. Using a gauge for a monotonically increasing value or a summary when cross-instance aggregation is needed leads to dashboards that silently lie.

children.title: Instant vs Range Vectors
children.description: PromQL distinguishes between a point-in-time snapshot (instant vector) and a window of samples (range vector), and functions like rate() require range vectors. Confusing the two produces cryptic type errors or, worse, silently incorrect alert expressions.

children.title: Cardinality and Label Design
children.description: Each unique combination of label values creates a new time series in memory; unbounded label cardinality from user IDs or UUIDs can crash a Prometheus server. Planning label schemas before instrumentation is the difference between a stable monitoring stack and an OOM loop.

children.title: Instrumenting with Client Libraries
children.description: Prometheus client libraries expose an HTTP endpoint that Prometheus scrapes, and the library handles thread-safe metric updates internally. Skipping instrumentation and relying solely on infrastructure metrics means you are blind to application-level failures like slow database queries.

children.title: Recording Rules for Efficiency
children.description: Recording rules pre-compute expensive PromQL expressions and store them as new time series, making dashboards and alerts faster. Without them, complex queries recalculated on every dashboard refresh or alert evaluation introduce latency that degrades Prometheus reliability.

children.title: Routing and Grouping
children.description: Alertmanager deduplicates, groups, and routes alerts to the right receivers; without routing rules, every alert goes to every team and alert fatigue sets in within days. A misconfigured grouping can collapse critical distinct failures into one alert that obscures scope.

children.title: Silences and Inhibitions
children.description: Silences suppress alerts during maintenance windows; inhibitions prevent lower-priority alerts from firing when a root-cause alert is already active. Ignoring these features means on-call engineers receive floods of symptom alerts during an outage, obscuring the actual cause.

sre.title: Dashboards and Visualization with Grafana
sre.description: This layer covers turning metrics into actionable dashboards. It matters because clear visualization is how teams spot trends and diagnose problems quickly.
sre.challenge: Build a Grafana dashboard that tracks the latency and error rate of your Go service.

children.title: Panel Types and When to Use Them
children.description: Choosing the wrong panel type — a graph for a single current value, or a stat panel for a trend — obscures the information operators need. A well-chosen panel type communicates status instantly; a wrong one requires staring and interpretation during an incident.

children.title: Dashboard Provisioning as Code
children.description: Grafana dashboards stored as JSON in Git and provisioned via config files survive cluster recreation and can be reviewed in pull requests. Manually created dashboards disappear when a Grafana instance is replaced and cannot be audited or rolled back.

children.title: Query Variables for Reusable Dashboards
children.description: Template variables let a single dashboard switch between environments, clusters, or services without duplication. Without variables, teams maintain one dashboard per service and version drift makes them useless within weeks.

children.title: Annotations for Context
children.description: Annotations overlay events — deployments, config changes, incidents — on time-series graphs, making it trivial to correlate a metric spike with a specific action. Without annotations, engineers spend the first minutes of every investigation manually cross-referencing deployment logs with metric timelines.

children.title: Error Budget Burn Dashboards
children.description: An error budget burn dashboard shows how fast the budget is being consumed relative to the safe burn rate, enabling teams to act before an SLO is missed rather than after. A dashboard that shows only raw error rate provides no sense of whether the current rate is sustainable.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — SLO Dashboard Examples

children.title: RED Method Dashboards
children.description: The RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration) provides a consistent three-panel dashboard structure for every service, making it fast to diagnose any service without learning a unique layout. Teams without a consistent method waste incident minutes orienting to unfamiliar dashboards.

children.title: RBAC for Dashboard Access
children.description: Grafana's role-based access control lets teams own their dashboards while keeping sensitive infrastructure metrics restricted to SREs. Without access control, developers accidentally modify production dashboards or see data they are not authorized to view.

children.title: Grafana Alerting
children.description: Grafana's unified alerting can route multi-data-source alerts through contact points and notification policies, keeping alert management in the same tool as visualization. Splitting alerting across Prometheus Alertmanager and Grafana without a unified policy creates gaps where alerts are never delivered.

sre.title: Service Level Objectives
sre.description: This layer focuses on defining and measuring SLOs and error budgets. It matters because SLOs translate reliability goals into concrete, enforceable targets.
sre.challenge: Define an SLO for your service and create burn-rate alerts that fire when the error budget depletes too fast.

children.title: Choosing Realistic SLO Targets
children.description: An SLO set too tight consumes the entire error budget with routine deployments, freezing feature development; set too loose it provides no reliability signal. The target should reflect the current measured baseline with a reasonable improvement trajectory rather than an aspirational number.

children.title: User Journey vs System SLIs
children.description: SLIs tied to user journeys (checkout success rate) are more meaningful than low-level infrastructure metrics (CPU idle) because they directly represent user experience. A service can show green infrastructure metrics while users experience total failure if the SLIs are chosen at the wrong layer.

children.title: Error Budget Calculation
children.description: The error budget is (1 - SLO target) multiplied by the measurement window, expressed as allowable downtime or bad request count. Getting the math wrong leads to teams thinking they have more budget than they do and shipping risky changes at the worst time.

children.title: Error Budget Policy Enforcement
children.description: An error budget policy defines what happens when the budget is depleted — feature freeze, mandatory reliability sprint, escalation to leadership — and gives the SLO teeth. Without a policy, the SLO is advisory and ignored the moment business pressure mounts.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — Error Budget Policy

children.title: Single Burn Rate vs Multi-Window Alerts
