children.description: A single burn rate threshold misses either fast-burning short incidents (too slow to page) or slow-burning long incidents (too noisy). Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting catches both by combining a short window for fast detection with a long window to confirm sustained problems.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — Alerting on SLOs

children.title: Alert Severity Tiers
children.description: Burn rates map naturally to severity: a rate that exhausts the budget in an hour demands an immediate page, while a rate exhausting it in three days warrants a ticket. Flattening all burn rates into the same severity creates alert fatigue that causes engineers to ignore genuinely critical pages.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — SLO Alert Severity

children.title: Reporting to Stakeholders
children.description: SLO status reports translate engineering reliability metrics into business language, building trust and justifying reliability investment. Without regular reporting, leadership bases reliability conversations on anecdote and support ticket volume rather than data.

children.title: SLO Review Cadence
children.description: SLOs should be reviewed quarterly to adjust targets based on actual user needs and system improvements — a 99.0% target set two years ago may now be appropriate to tighten or relax. Stale SLOs that no longer reflect user expectations provide false confidence about service health.

sre.title: Alerting and On-Call with PagerDuty
sre.description: This layer teaches how to route alerts and run sustainable on-call rotations. It matters because well-designed alerting protects both systems and the humans who operate them.
sre.challenge: Wire Alertmanager to PagerDuty so your burn-rate alerts page an on-call schedule with escalation.

children.title: Routing Trees and Receivers
children.description: Alertmanager matches alerts against a routing tree in order, sending each alert to the first matching receiver — misconfigurations silently drop alerts to a default catch-all receiver that may be an empty sink. Testing routing logic before a real incident is the only way to confirm alerts will reach the right team.

children.title: Grouping and Repeat Intervals
children.description: Grouping collapses related alerts into one notification and repeat intervals control how often Alertmanager re-notifies for an ongoing firing alert. Without grouping, a cascading failure generates hundreds of individual pages; without repeat intervals, an alert fires once and is forgotten if the responder misses it.

children.title: Escalation Policies
children.description: An escalation policy defines who is paged first, how long before escalating, and who receives the page if no one acknowledges. Without escalation policies, a missed primary page means an incident goes unaddressed until someone notices in a chat channel hours later.

children.title: On-Call Schedules
children.description: PagerDuty on-call schedules rotate responsibility across team members and define coverage gaps that must be filled by override or escalation. Without a formal schedule, on-call responsibility defaults to whoever responds first, leading to burnout of the most conscientious engineers.

children.title: Actionable vs Noisy Alerts
children.description: Every alert that pages a human must require immediate action and be impossible to auto-remediate — anything else is noise. A pager full of informational alerts trains engineers to ignore it, and the one true emergency gets missed.

resources.label: Google SRE Book — Monitoring Distributed Systems

children.title: On-Call Handoffs
children.description: A structured handoff document transfers context about open incidents, elevated risk systems, and pending changes to the incoming engineer. Without handoffs, the incoming engineer is blind to ongoing investigations and may reopen resolved issues or miss follow-up actions.

children.title: Maintenance Windows
children.description: Maintenance windows suppress expected alerts during planned changes so on-call engineers are not paged for known degradation. Missing a maintenance window causes unnecessary pages; an overly broad window masks real incidents running concurrently with maintenance.

children.title: Alert Fatigue Prevention
children.description: Alert fatigue occurs when engineers receive so many pages that they become desensitized and stop investigating carefully. Tracking alert volume and false-positive rates as SLIs of the alerting system itself creates accountability for keeping pages meaningful.

sre.title: Logging and Distributed Tracing
sre.description: This layer covers collecting logs and traces to understand request flows. It matters because metrics alone cannot explain why a specific request failed.
sre.challenge: Add OpenTelemetry tracing to your Go service and correlate a slow trace with its logs and metrics.

children.title: Structured vs Unstructured Logs
children.description: Structured logs emit key-value pairs in a consistent format (JSON) so log aggregators can index and query specific fields without fragile regex parsing. Unstructured logs work fine locally but become unsearchable at scale, making incident investigation dependent on guessing the right grep string.

children.title: Loki Log Aggregation
children.description: Loki aggregates logs with a label-based indexing approach similar to Prometheus, keeping storage costs low by not indexing log content. Treating Loki like Elasticsearch and adding high-cardinality labels to every log stream defeats this design and causes ingestion failures.

children.title: Traces, Spans, and Context Propagation
children.description: A trace is a directed acyclic graph of spans, each representing a unit of work, linked by a trace context header propagated across service boundaries. Without context propagation between services, traces break into disconnected fragments that cannot show the full request path.

children.title: Sampling Strategies
children.description: Sampling determines what fraction of traces are recorded; head-based sampling decides at request start, while tail-based sampling can selectively keep interesting traces (errors, slow requests) after completion. Sampling 100% of traces at high throughput is cost-prohibitive; sampling 0.01% misses most errors.

children.title: OTel SDK vs Auto-Instrumentation
children.description: OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation agents inject tracing into common frameworks with zero code changes; manual SDK instrumentation adds spans for custom business logic. Using only auto-instrumentation misses the internal service logic that causes most latency; using only manual instrumentation misses third-party library calls.

children.title: Collector Configuration
children.description: The OpenTelemetry Collector is a vendor-agnostic pipeline that receives, processes, and exports telemetry to multiple backends simultaneously. Sending telemetry directly from every application to a backend tightly couples your instrumentation to a specific vendor and makes backend migration painful.

children.title: Trace-to-Log Correlation
children.description: Injecting the trace ID into log entries allows engineers to jump from a slow span in a trace directly to the log lines emitted during that span. Without correlation, root-cause analysis requires manually aligning timestamps across tools, which is slow and error-prone under incident pressure.

children.title: Exemplars Linking Metrics to Traces
children.description: Prometheus exemplars embed a trace ID as metadata on individual metric samples, enabling a direct link from a high-latency histogram bucket to the exact trace that caused it. Without exemplars, engineers know a p99 spike occurred but must hunt through traces manually to find the culprit request.

sre.title: Incident Management
sre.description: This layer focuses on responding to and learning from production incidents. It matters because how an organization handles incidents shapes its long-term reliability.
sre.challenge: Run a simulated incident using a runbook and write a blameless postmortem with action items.

children.title: Incident Commander Role
children.description: The incident commander owns the response process, coordinates communication, and makes decisions under uncertainty — they do not fix the problem themselves. Without a clear commander, every engineer pulls in a different direction and the group converges on the wrong fix while the outage drags on.

children.title: Severity Classification
children.description: Severity levels define the expected response speed, communication cadence, and escalation path for an incident, removing ambiguity under stress. Without agreed severity definitions, one engineer's SEV2 is another's SEV5, causing under-reaction to critical outages and over-reaction to minor blips.

children.title: Writing Actionable Runbooks
children.description: A runbook is a step-by-step procedure an on-call engineer follows during a specific alert, including diagnostic commands and remediation steps. Runbooks written as vague guidelines require expert knowledge to interpret; they must be precise enough for a junior engineer at 3 AM to follow successfully.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — On-Call and Runbooks

children.title: Runbook Automation
children.description: Automated runbooks execute diagnostic and remediation steps programmatically when an alert fires, reducing mean time to resolution without human intervention. A runbook that stays as a wiki document is only as fast as the engineer reading it; automation collapses hours of manual steps to seconds.

children.title: Postmortem Structure
children.description: A blameless postmortem documents the timeline, contributing factors, customer impact, and action items without attributing fault to individuals. A poorly structured postmortem that names individuals as the root cause destroys psychological safety and ensures the next engineer hides the next near-miss.

children.title: Action Item Tracking
children.description: Postmortem action items must be assigned to owners with deadlines and tracked to completion, not buried in a document that is never revisited. Untracked action items mean the same incident recurs with a fresh postmortem and no systemic improvement.

resources.label: Google SRE Workbook — Postmortem Action Items

children.title: Stakeholder Updates
children.description: Regular status updates to stakeholders at defined intervals prevent leadership from interrupting the engineering response to ask for status. A responder fielding questions from seven Slack threads simultaneously cannot focus on mitigation.

children.title: Incident Channels and War Rooms
children.description: Dedicated incident communication channels separate the response conversation from general team noise and preserve a searchable timeline for the postmortem. Coordinating an incident across general team channels means context is lost in unrelated conversation and the timeline is impossible to reconstruct afterward.

sre.title: Reliability Engineering in Go
sre.description: This layer teaches building resilient services and tooling in Go. It matters because SREs often write automation and reliability features directly into services.
sre.challenge: Add timeouts, retries with backoff, and a circuit breaker to your Go service and expose their metrics.

children.title: Timeouts and Context Cancellation
children.description: Every outbound call in a Go service must have a deadline via context.WithTimeout; without it, a slow downstream dependency holds goroutines indefinitely until the entire service runs out of file descriptors. A service with proper timeouts degrades gracefully when dependencies are slow; one without will cascade-fail.

children.title: Retry with Exponential Backoff
children.description: Retries recover transient failures but exponential backoff with jitter prevents retry storms that amplify load on an already-struggling dependency. A retry loop with no backoff and no jitter turns a partial outage into a full one.

children.title: Circuit Breaker State Machine
children.description: A circuit breaker transitions through closed (normal), open (failing fast), and half-open (probing recovery) states to prevent a service from repeatedly hammering a broken dependency. Without circuit breakers, timeouts stack up, goroutine pools exhaust, and the calling service fails completely due to a partial downstream issue.

children.title: Bulkhead Isolation
children.description: Bulkheads partition goroutine pools or connection limits so that a misbehaving subsystem cannot exhaust resources shared by healthy subsystems. Without bulkheads, one slow endpoint can consume all worker goroutines and cause the entire service to appear unresponsive.

children.title: Liveness vs Readiness Probes
children.description: Liveness probes determine if a process should be restarted; readiness probes determine if it should receive traffic. Conflating the two leads to Kubernetes restarting healthy pods that are temporarily unable to serve (during startup or dependency unavailability) and causing unnecessary disruption.

children.title: Graceful Shutdown
children.description: A graceful shutdown drains in-flight requests before the process exits, preventing clients from receiving connection-reset errors during deployments. Without it, every rolling update causes a burst of 5xx errors visible in user-facing SLO metrics.

resources.label: Go — Signal Handling and Graceful Shutdown

children.title: Token Bucket Rate Limiting
children.description: Token bucket rate limiting allows bursts up to a configured maximum while enforcing a sustained request rate, protecting downstream services from sudden spikes. A service with no rate limiting is vulnerable to a single misbehaving client consuming all capacity and causing degradation for everyone else.

children.title: Load Shedding Under Pressure
children.description: Load shedding deliberately rejects excess requests with 503 responses when a service is overloaded, preserving capacity for requests that can be served well. A service that accepts all requests under overload degrades for everyone; one that sheds load degrades gracefully for only the excess.

sre.title: Capacity Planning and Chaos Engineering
sre.description: This layer covers forecasting demand and proactively testing failure modes. It matters because resilient systems are validated under stress rather than assumed.
sre.challenge: Run a chaos experiment that injects latency and verify your SLOs and alerts behave as expected.

children.title: Forecasting from Metrics
children.description: Capacity forecasting fits growth trends onto historical Prometheus metrics to project when a resource will be exhausted, enabling proactive provisioning before users experience degradation. Without forecasting, teams provision reactively after an incident and frequently over-provision out of fear.

resources.label: Google SRE Book — Forecasting and Capacity Planning

children.title: Load Testing and Benchmarking
children.description: Load tests establish the actual throughput and latency profile of a service under controlled conditions, providing the data that capacity forecasting extrapolates from. Forecasting without load test baselines produces projections anchored to unknown real-world behavior.

children.title: Chaos Hypotheses
children.description: A chaos experiment starts with a specific hypothesis — 'if pod X fails, the service will still respond within SLO' — so the result either confirms or refutes a concrete assumption. Running chaos without hypotheses produces random destruction with no learning.

children.title: Failure Injection with Chaos Mesh
children.description: Chaos Mesh injects pod failures, network partitions, latency, and disk errors into Kubernetes workloads via Kubernetes CRDs, enabling reproducible chaos experiments as code. Manual chaos (pulling network cables) cannot be reproduced precisely and leaves no audit trail for postmortem reference.

children.title: Horizontal vs Vertical Autoscaling
children.description: Horizontal autoscaling adds more instances to handle load and is zero-downtime; vertical autoscaling resizes existing instances and typically requires a restart. Choosing vertical scaling for stateless services that need fast burst handling leads to downtime during peak traffic.

children.title: Custom Metrics Autoscaling
children.description: Scaling on CPU and memory is a proxy for business load; scaling on queue depth, RPS, or active sessions directly responds to the work that needs doing. A service that scales on CPU may be idle while its queue fills because the bottleneck is network I/O, not CPU.

children.title: Game Day Planning
children.description: A game day is a scheduled, controlled rehearsal of a failure scenario with the full response team, validating that runbooks, alerting, and escalation paths work before a real incident. Skipping game days means the first time a team experiences a major failure mode is during an actual production outage.

children.title: Measuring Resilience Improvements
children.description: Game day results should feed a resilience scorecard tracking MTTR, time to detect, and runbook effectiveness over time so improvements are visible. Without measurement, game days feel productive but provide no evidence of organizational learning.

sre.title: Operating Reliability at Scale
sre.description: This layer covers running observability and reliability practices across a large production fleet. It matters because reliability programs must scale with the organization without collapsing under toil.
sre.challenge: Stand up a federated, long-term observability stack and produce a reliability scorecard for multiple services.

children.title: Prometheus Retention Limits
children.description: Local Prometheus storage is limited to days or weeks; without a long-term backend, capacity forecasting, trend analysis, and SLO reporting lose historical context. An organization relying on 15-day Prometheus retention cannot answer questions about seasonality or year-over-year growth.

children.title: Thanos Architecture
children.description: Thanos extends Prometheus with object storage upload, a global query layer across multiple Prometheus instances, and long-term compaction with downsampling. Without understanding Thanos's sidecar vs receiver deployment models, teams end up with duplicate data or missed metrics during network partitions.

children.title: Federated Prometheus
children.description: Prometheus federation allows a global Prometheus instance to scrape aggregated metrics from cluster-level Prometheus instances, providing a single pane across many environments. Federating the wrong metrics (high-cardinality raw series rather than aggregated recording rules) overloads the global instance.

children.title: Cost-Aware Observability
children.description: Observability infrastructure (storage, ingestion, queries) has real cost that scales with cardinality, retention, and query volume; without cost awareness, monitoring spend can exceed the cost of the services being monitored. Downsampling, recording rules, and label cardinality budgets keep observability economically sustainable.

children.title: Measuring Toil Systematically
children.description: Toil must be quantified — hours per week, tickets per engineer, manual steps per deploy — before it can be prioritized for elimination. Without measurement, toil reduction is driven by whoever complains loudest rather than where engineering time is actually draining.

children.title: Reliability Scorecards
children.description: A reliability scorecard grades each service on SLO compliance, toil level, incident count, and postmortem action item completion, providing a consistent cross-service view for leadership. Without scorecards, reliability investment is invisible and underfunded until a high-profile outage forces the conversation.

children.title: SRE Engagement Model
children.description: An SRE engagement model defines the criteria for SRE teams to onboard, support, and hand back services, preventing the SRE function from becoming a permanent ops team for every service. Without a clear engagement model, SRE becomes a bottleneck and teams lose the incentive to own their own reliability.

resources.label: Google SRE Book — The SRE Engagement Model

children.title: Reliability Review Cadence
children.description: Regular reliability reviews examine SLO trends, postmortem action item completion, and toil metrics to drive a continuous improvement cycle rather than reacting only to incidents. Organizations without a review cadence improve after large incidents but drift back to fragility between them.

infra.title: Linux and Infrastructure Basics
infra.description: This layer builds the operating system and networking foundation every infrastructure engineer needs. It matters because infrastructure tooling ultimately manages Linux hosts, networks, and processes.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana Linux Crash Course

infra.challenge: Write a Bash script that provisions and configures a service on a fresh Linux virtual machine.

children.title: Filesystem and Permissions
children.description: Linux uses a hierarchical filesystem where every resource is a file. Misunderstanding permissions (user, group, other) leads to services failing silently or running with dangerous over-privilege, both of which are common sources of production incidents.

children.title: Process and Service Management with systemd
children.description: systemd controls how services start, stop, and recover on failure. Without understanding unit files and journalctl, debugging a crashed daemon becomes guesswork and you lose visibility into what the OS is actually doing.

children.title: Bash Variables, Loops, and Functions
children.description: Bash is the glue that ties infrastructure tasks together. Without solid scripting skills you will write brittle one-liners that break on edge cases, making automation unreliable and difficult for teammates to maintain.

resources.label: ShellCheck — Static Analysis for Shell Scripts

children.title: SSH and Remote Access
children.description: SSH is the primary protocol for managing remote servers. Misconfigured key-based auth or misunderstood agent forwarding creates security holes; not knowing tunneling prevents you from reaching private services safely.

children.title: IP Addressing and Subnetting
children.description: Every cloud resource sits inside a network range. Without understanding CIDR notation, subnet masks, and routing tables, you will misconfigure VPCs, create overlapping address spaces, and wonder why services cannot reach each other.

children.title: Package Management
children.description: apt, yum, and dnf install and update every software dependency on a Linux host. Skipping version pinning or ignoring held packages causes configuration drift across environments and mysterious breakages after updates.

children.title: Git Branching and Pull Requests
children.description: Infrastructure code lives in Git just like application code. Without disciplined branching and code review, unreviewed changes reach production directly, removing the audit trail needed to understand what changed and when an incident started.

children.title: Commit History as Documentation
children.description: Meaningful commit messages and small, focused commits make it possible to bisect regressions and understand intent months later. Treating commits as throwaway saves time now but creates expensive archaeology during incidents.

resources.label: How to Write a Git Commit Message

infra.title: Infrastructure as Code Concepts
infra.description: This layer introduces the principles of declaring infrastructure in version-controlled code. It matters because manual infrastructure changes are error-prone and impossible to reproduce reliably.
infra.challenge: Use Terraform to declare and create a basic virtual network and compute instance from code.

children.title: Declarative vs Imperative Infrastructure
children.description: Declarative IaC describes the desired end state and lets the tool figure out how to reach it, while imperative scripting describes every step. Conflating the two causes idempotency failures where re-running a script breaks things it already configured correctly.

children.title: Idempotency and Desired State
children.description: An idempotent operation produces the same result whether run once or ten times. Without this property, automation is unsafe to re-run after partial failures, turning every incident into a high-stakes manual intervention.

children.title: Infrastructure State Files
children.description: Terraform stores a map between your code and real cloud resources in a state file. If that file is lost, corrupted, or out of sync due to manual changes, Terraform cannot safely plan and may try to recreate or destroy live resources.

children.title: Configuration Drift
children.description: Drift happens when the actual state of infrastructure diverges from what IaC declares, usually due to manual console changes. Undetected drift leads to environments that cannot be reproduced and failures that appear only in production.

children.title: Terraform Providers
children.description: Providers are plugins that translate Terraform resources into API calls for a specific platform. Choosing the wrong provider version or not pinning versions causes unexpected breaking changes when the provider is upgraded.

children.title: Plan and Apply Workflow
children.description: Running terraform plan before apply shows exactly what will change and why, acting as a mandatory safety check. Skipping plan and applying directly, especially in shared environments, is how accidental deletions of production resources happen.

children.title: Repository Structure for IaC
children.description: How you organize Terraform directories determines how safely you can make changes across environments. A flat, unmodularized repo makes it easy to accidentally apply a change to production when targeting staging.

resources.label: Terraform: Up and Running — Repo Layout

children.title: Secrets in IaC Code
children.description: Credentials, tokens, and passwords must never be committed to Git, even in private repositories. Secret scanning tools and vault integrations exist precisely because developers routinely make this mistake, leading to credential leaks.

infra.title: Provisioning with Terraform
infra.description: This layer deepens Terraform usage for real provisioning workflows. It matters because Terraform is the dominant tool for cloud infrastructure provisioning.
infra.challenge: Parameterize your Terraform configuration with variables and outputs to deploy to two environments.

children.title: Variables, Locals, and Outputs
children.description: Variables make Terraform configurations reusable across environments; outputs expose values to other modules or pipelines. Without understanding type constraints and validation, callers pass wrong values silently and downstream resources get misconfigured.

children.title: Expressions and Functions
children.description: HCL expressions allow dynamic values — string interpolation, conditionals, for loops, and built-in functions. Misusing them produces configurations that appear valid but generate incorrect resource arguments only visible at apply time.

children.title: Implicit vs Explicit Dependencies
children.description: Terraform builds a dependency graph to determine creation order. Missing a dependency either causes a race condition at apply time or requires you to add an explicit depends_on, which is easy to forget and produces cryptic errors.

children.title: Lifecycle Rules
children.description: Lifecycle blocks control whether Terraform replaces, ignores changes to, or prevents deletion of a resource. Getting this wrong destroys stateful resources like databases during routine updates, causing data loss.

children.title: Reading Existing Resources
children.description: Data sources let Terraform read resources it does not manage — existing AMIs, VPCs, or certificates — without importing them into state. Hard-coding IDs instead breaks cross-environment portability and makes configurations impossible to reuse.

children.title: Remote Data with terraform_remote_state
children.description: terraform_remote_state lets one stack read outputs from another without tight coupling through a shared backend. Not using this forces teams to hard-code cross-stack values, breaking the boundary between independently managed modules.

children.title: Environment Isolation with Workspaces
children.description: Terraform workspaces store separate state files under a single configuration, allowing dev and prod to diverge safely. Using a single workspace for all environments causes plan output to mix changes across environments and risks applying staging changes to production.

children.title: Provisioners and Their Risks
children.description: Provisioners run scripts on resources after creation, but they run only once and failures leave state inconsistent. Relying on provisioners for configuration management bypasses idempotency entirely; prefer Ansible or cloud-init instead.

infra.title: Configuration Management with Ansible
infra.description: This layer covers automating server configuration after provisioning. It matters because consistent configuration prevents snowflake servers and configuration drift.
infra.challenge: Write an Ansible playbook that configures and hardens the servers Terraform provisioned.

children.title: Static and Dynamic Inventories
children.description: Inventories tell Ansible which hosts to target and how to group them. A static inventory falls out of sync as cloud instances scale; a dynamic inventory plugin queries the cloud API instead, keeping automation accurate without manual updates.

children.title: Host Variables and Group Variables
children.description: host_vars and group_vars let you customize behavior per host or environment without duplicating playbooks. Ignoring this pattern leads to environment-specific logic embedded directly in tasks, making playbooks hard to maintain and error-prone.

children.title: Task Modules and Idempotency
children.description: Ansible modules are the atomic actions in a playbook. Using the shell or command module instead of purpose-built modules (copy, file, service) sacrifices idempotency because shell commands do not check current state before acting.

children.title: Handlers and Notifications
children.description: Handlers run only when notified by a task that changed state — the correct way to restart a service after a config change. Using a direct restart task instead causes the service to restart every run regardless of whether anything changed, causing unnecessary downtime.

children.title: Jinja2 Templates
children.description: Ansible uses Jinja2 to render configuration files dynamically from variables. Not understanding template rendering order or filter syntax produces config files with literal {{ variable }} strings shipped to servers, breaking application startup.

children.title: Ansible Vault for Secrets
children.description: Ansible Vault encrypts sensitive values so they can be committed safely to Git. Storing passwords as plaintext in vars files or inventory is a common mistake that leaks credentials in any shared or audited repository.

children.title: Running One-Off Tasks
children.description: Ansible ad hoc commands let you run a single module across your fleet without writing a playbook — useful for gathering facts, checking status, or applying a hotfix. Not knowing this forces engineers to SSH into every server individually.

children.title: Privilege Escalation
children.description: become and become_user allow Ansible to execute tasks as root or another user. Missing this causes tasks like package installation or service management to fail silently with permission errors rather than helpful messages.

infra.title: Reusable Modules and Roles
infra.description: This layer teaches structuring infrastructure code for reuse and maintainability. It matters because modular code scales across teams and reduces duplication.
infra.challenge: Refactor your Terraform and Ansible code into reusable modules and roles consumed by multiple stacks.

children.title: Module Structure and Composition
children.description: A Terraform module is a reusable unit with its own inputs, outputs, and resources. Without modules, every environment duplicates the same resource definitions, and a single bug must be fixed in dozens of places instead of one.

children.title: Versioning Shared Modules
children.description: Pinning module versions prevents a published change from silently breaking every consumer. Using an unversioned source reference means any update to the module is instantly applied everywhere, removing the ability to test upgrades safely.

children.title: Role Directory Structure
children.description: Ansible roles enforce a standard layout of tasks, handlers, variables, templates, and files. Not following this convention makes roles hard for other engineers to understand and prevents Ansible Galaxy from distributing them correctly.

children.title: Ansible Galaxy and Role Distribution
children.description: Galaxy is the community hub for sharing Ansible roles and collections. Not using it means rewriting well-tested roles from scratch; not understanding requirements.yml prevents teams from pinning role versions and controlling updates.

children.title: Terraform Variable Validation
children.description: Validation blocks let you reject invalid inputs before plan even runs, producing clear error messages rather than cryptic API errors. Without them, a typo in an environment name reaches the cloud provider and returns a confusing response.

children.title: DRY Infrastructure Patterns
children.description: Don't Repeat Yourself applies to infrastructure as much as application code. Duplicated resource blocks across environments guarantee that security patches, naming changes, or configuration updates are applied inconsistently.

children.title: Using the Public Terraform Registry
children.description: The Terraform Registry hosts thousands of community and official modules. Using them saves significant time, but not reviewing their source code or pinning a version means you inherit breaking changes or vulnerabilities introduced by the module maintainer.

children.title: Private Module Registries
children.description: Organizations publish internal modules to private registries in Terraform Cloud or Artifactory. Without a private registry, sharing modules relies on Git refs, which lack discovery, access control, and the semantic versioning contract consumers expect.

infra.title: Programmatic IaC with Pulumi
infra.description: This layer introduces defining infrastructure using general-purpose programming languages. It matters because programmatic IaC unlocks loops, conditionals, and richer abstractions.
infra.challenge: Reimplement one of your Terraform stacks in Pulumi using a real programming language.

children.title: Stacks and Configuration
children.description: A Pulumi stack is an independent deployment target — dev, staging, production. Without isolating stacks, a single state file covers all environments, and a failed update in one environment can corrupt state for another.

children.title: Resource Graph and Dependencies
children.description: Pulumi builds an implicit dependency graph from how outputs are passed between resources. Failing to use outputs as inputs — instead using string literals — breaks the graph and causes race conditions where a resource is referenced before it exists.

children.title: TypeScript for Infrastructure
children.description: Pulumi TypeScript gives you type-checked resource properties and IDE autocompletion. Without this, typos in property names silently produce undefined values that only surface as API errors during apply, wasting debugging time.

children.title: Reusable Component Resources
children.description: Pulumi component resources let you bundle multiple cloud resources into a logical, reusable class. Without them, duplicating the same set of S3 + CloudFront + IAM resources across projects becomes unmanageable and inconsistent.

children.title: When to Choose Pulumi
children.description: Pulumi excels when infrastructure requires complex logic — dynamic resource counts, conditional creation, or rich abstractions — that HCL cannot express cleanly. Choosing it for simple flat infrastructure adds SDK overhead without benefit.

children.title: Terraform Interoperability
children.description: Pulumi can read Terraform state files and use Terraform providers, enabling gradual migration. Not understanding this makes teams feel forced into a big-bang rewrite, when incremental coexistence is usually the safer path.

children.title: Loops and Conditionals in IaC
children.description: General-purpose languages let you iterate over lists and branch on conditions without the limitations of HCL count and for_each. Misusing these constructs changes resource addresses on each run, causing Terraform-equivalent state churn.

children.title: Testing Infrastructure Code
children.description: Pulumi supports unit tests via standard test frameworks in TypeScript, Python, and Go. Untested infrastructure code accumulates regressions just like application code, but bugs here take down production rather than just one feature.

infra.title: State, Secrets, and Remote Backends
infra.description: This layer covers managing infrastructure state and sensitive values safely. It matters because corrupted state or leaked secrets can cause outages and breaches.
infra.challenge: Migrate Terraform state to a remote locking backend and inject secrets from Vault at apply time.

children.title: S3 and DynamoDB State Locking
children.description: Storing Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking prevents two engineers from applying simultaneously, which corrupts state and can create duplicate or orphaned resources. Running without locking in a team context is a guaranteed eventual incident.

children.title: Importing Existing Resources
children.description: terraform import pulls manually created resources into state so Terraform can manage them. Without this skill, brownfield infrastructure stays outside IaC control permanently, accumulating ungoverned drift.

children.title: HashiCorp Vault Architecture
children.description: Vault centralizes secret storage, rotation, and access control with a full audit log. Without it, secrets live in environment variables, config files, or CI variables — all of which are hard to rotate and easy to leak.

children.title: Dynamic Credentials with Vault
children.description: Vault's dynamic secrets engine generates short-lived, scoped credentials on demand for databases, AWS, and other providers. Without dynamic credentials, teams share long-lived passwords that cannot be safely rotated without downtime.

children.title: State Backup and Versioning
children.description: Enabling versioning on the S3 bucket that holds Terraform state means corrupted or accidentally deleted state files can be restored. Teams that skip this discover they need it only after an incident when restoring is also under time pressure.

children.title: Sensitive Values in State
children.description: Terraform state files may contain plaintext secrets even if marked sensitive in code, because state is a raw snapshot of resource attributes. Not encrypting state at rest and in transit exposes credentials to anyone with bucket read access.

children.title: Vault Policies and Auth Methods
children.description: Vault policies define what paths a token can read or write; auth methods (AppRole, Kubernetes, AWS IAM) determine how callers prove identity. Overly broad policies or using root tokens in automation defeats the purpose of having Vault.

children.title: Vault in Terraform Pipelines
children.description: The Vault provider for Terraform fetches secrets at plan/apply time without hardcoding them. Not using this integration means secrets travel through CI environment variables, bypassing Vault's audit log and rotation policies.

infra.title: Infrastructure CI/CD and Testing
infra.description: This layer teaches automating and validating infrastructure changes in pipelines. It matters because applying infrastructure by hand at scale is slow and risky.
infra.challenge: Build a pipeline that lints, plans, tests, and applies your Terraform with a manual approval gate.

children.title: Terraform in CI with GitHub Actions
children.description: Running terraform plan on pull requests and terraform apply on merge gives infrastructure changes the same review gate as application code. Without this, changes are applied manually and inconsistently, removing the audit trail and increasing human error.

children.title: Approval Workflows
children.description: Manual approval gates in CI block destructive changes (like replacing a database) until a human confirms. Automating apply without approvals means any merged PR can destroy production resources without a review checkpoint.

children.title: Terraform Sentinel and OPA
children.description: Policy as code tools evaluate Terraform plans against rules before apply — blocking public S3 buckets, unencrypted volumes, or missing tags. Without enforcement at the pipeline level, policy violations reach production and require reactive cleanup.

children.title: Linting and Formatting
children.description: terraform fmt enforces consistent HCL style; tflint catches provider-specific mistakes before plan. Skipping these lets style inconsistencies accumulate and allows common mistakes like invalid instance types to only surface at apply time.

children.title: Terratest for Integration Testing
children.description: Terratest deploys real infrastructure in a test account, validates it, and tears it down automatically. Without integration tests, a module may pass plan but fail at apply in ways only caught in production when it is too late.

children.title: Terraform Native Test Framework
children.description: Terraform 1.6+ ships a built-in test framework that runs plan or apply for module scenarios and asserts on outputs. This lowers the barrier to testing without external Go knowledge required by Terratest.

children.title: Ephemeral Infrastructure for PRs
children.description: Creating a short-lived environment per pull request lets developers test infrastructure changes in isolation. Without preview environments, every change must be merged and tested in shared staging, causing conflicts and long feedback cycles.

children.title: Cost Estimation in CI
children.description: Infracost runs alongside terraform plan and shows the monthly cost delta of a change before it merges. Without this, engineers routinely provision expensive resources (large instance types, NAT gateways) without realizing the cost impact.

infra.title: Multi-Environment and Multi-Cloud
infra.description: This layer covers structuring infrastructure across environments and providers. It matters because real organizations run separate environments and sometimes multiple clouds.
infra.challenge: Lay out dev, staging, and prod environments from shared modules with isolated state per environment.

children.title: Directory Layouts for Multi-Environment IaC
children.description: Separate directories per environment with isolated state files prevent a plan for dev from touching prod state. Sharing a single directory with workspace-only separation is fragile — the wrong terraform workspace select before apply can target production accidentally.

resources.label: Terraform: Up and Running — Environment Isolation

children.title: Promoting Changes Across Stages
children.description: Infrastructure promotion means applying the same configuration to dev, then staging, then prod — validating at each step. Without a controlled promotion flow, changes that break staging are often cherry-picked into production without being fully understood.

children.title: Provider Abstractions
children.description: Using Terraform modules that wrap provider-specific details lets code express intent (a database, a network) rather than AWS or GCP specifics. Without abstractions, switching providers requires rewriting all resource blocks rather than swapping a module source.

children.title: Cross-Cloud Networking
children.description: Connecting workloads across AWS and GCP requires VPN gateways, BGP peering, or third-party solutions. Without understanding the latency, cost, and security implications of cross-cloud traffic, multi-cloud architecture creates hidden bottlenecks.

children.title: Resource Tagging Strategies
children.description: Consistent tags (environment, team, cost-center) are how you attribute cloud spend, enforce policies, and filter resources at scale. Without enforced tagging, cost allocation becomes guesswork and governance policies cannot be applied selectively.

children.title: Cost Governance Across Environments
children.description: Each environment should have budget alerts and cost visibility tied to its tags. Without per-environment budgets, runaway dev resources silently inflate the bill and the first sign is a surprise invoice rather than an alert.

children.title: Stack Configuration Files
children.description: Pulumi stores per-stack configuration in Pulumi.<stack>.yaml files committed to Git. Sharing configuration values across stacks through hardcoded strings instead of stack config breaks environment isolation and makes environments diverge silently.

children.title: Stack References
children.description: Pulumi StackReferences let one stack consume outputs from another without tight code coupling. Not using them forces teams to hardcode resource IDs or ARNs, which diverge across environments and require manual updates on every change.

infra.title: Operating Infrastructure in Production
infra.description: This layer covers running, scaling, and recovering infrastructure reliably in production. It matters because infrastructure must remain stable, recoverable, and compliant under real workloads.
infra.challenge: Operate your full IaC stack with automated drift detection, backups, and a documented recovery runbook.

children.title: Automated Drift Detection
children.description: Running terraform plan on a schedule detects when real infrastructure diverges from state without waiting for a human to notice. Undetected drift accumulates over weeks and produces confusing plan output that blocks routine changes.

children.title: Drift Remediation Strategies
children.description: When drift is found you can re-apply IaC to restore desired state, or import the change into state if it was intentional. Without a documented remediation policy, engineers make ad hoc choices that leave state inconsistent or override valid manual fixes.

children.title: Infrastructure Backup Strategies
children.description: IaC code in Git is your primary recovery artifact — but state files, database snapshots, and AMIs are equally critical. Losing state without a backup forces a full import of all resources, which can take days for large environments.

children.title: Recovery Runbooks
children.description: A runbook documents the exact steps to restore infrastructure after a failure. Without tested runbooks, recovery during an incident relies on individual knowledge that may not be available when the engineer is asleep or has left the company.

children.title: Scaling Infrastructure with IaC
children.description: Capacity changes — larger instance types, more nodes, additional regions — should flow through IaC and pipelines, not the console. Ad hoc console scaling creates drift and prevents reproducibility of the scaled configuration after an incident.

children.title: Maintenance Windows and Patching
children.description: Planned maintenance windows for OS patches and provider upgrades prevent surprise reboots and API deprecations from affecting availability. Skipping scheduled patching means accumulating vulnerabilities that eventually require emergency downtime.

children.title: Compliance Auditing
children.description: Tools like AWS Config and tfsec continuously evaluate resources against compliance rules. Without automated auditing, compliance is a periodic manual exercise that misses violations introduced between audits.

children.title: Cost Optimization at Scale
children.description: Right-sizing instances, deleting unused resources, and purchasing savings plans can cut cloud bills by 30-60%. Without ongoing cost reviews tied to tagged environments, waste accumulates invisibly and is attributed to the wrong teams.

kubernetes.title: Containers and Docker Fundamentals
kubernetes.description: This layer establishes the container fundamentals that Kubernetes builds upon. It matters because you cannot operate Kubernetes well without understanding images, containers, and isolation.
kubernetes.challenge: Containerize a small web application and run it with a database using Docker Compose.

children.title: Linux Namespaces
children.description: Namespaces partition kernel resources so each container sees its own PID tree, network stack, and mount points. Without this mental model you will misdiagnose networking failures and misunderstand why a process visible in one container is invisible in another.

children.title: cgroups and Resource Limits
children.description: Control groups enforce CPU and memory ceilings on containers. If you skip this, you will not understand why Kubernetes OOMKills pods or why a noisy neighbor can starve your workload even inside separate containers.

children.title: Multi-Stage Builds
children.description: Multi-stage Dockerfiles compile code in a fat builder image and copy only the binary into a minimal runtime image. Skipping this produces images ten times larger than needed, increasing pull times and attack surface in production.

children.title: Layer Caching
children.description: Docker reuses unchanged layers from its build cache, making rebuilds near-instant when only source code changes. Ordering instructions incorrectly — such as copying all source before installing dependencies — busts the cache on every commit and wastes CI minutes.

children.title: OCI Image Spec
children.description: The Open Container Initiative image specification defines a common format so images built with Docker, Podman, or Buildah run on any compliant runtime. Ignoring this leads to portability surprises when switching runtimes or registries.

children.title: Container Security Baseline
children.description: Running containers as root, with a writable filesystem, or with excessive Linux capabilities creates exploitable attack paths. Understanding the security baseline — rootless execution, read-only FS, dropped capabilities — is mandatory before pushing containers to shared clusters.

children.title: Docker Compose Networking
children.description: Compose creates a private DNS network per project so services reach each other by name. Misunderstanding this — for example, using localhost — is the most common reason multi-container local setups fail before you ever touch Kubernetes.

children.title: Volume Mounts vs Bind Mounts
children.description: Named volumes are managed by Docker and survive container recreation; bind mounts expose host paths directly. Choosing wrong causes data loss on container restart or breaks portability between developer machines.

kubernetes.title: Kubernetes Core Concepts
kubernetes.description: This layer introduces the Kubernetes architecture and core objects. It matters because pods, services, and the control plane are the vocabulary of all later work.
kubernetes.challenge: Deploy your containerized app to a local cluster using Deployments and expose it with a Service.

children.title: etcd as the Source of Truth
children.description: etcd is the distributed key-value store that holds all cluster state. Every API server write goes to etcd first; if etcd is unhealthy the cluster cannot schedule or update anything. Understanding this explains why etcd backup is a first-class production concern.

children.title: The API Server and Admission Chain
children.description: Every kubectl command hits the API server, which runs authentication, authorization, and admission webhooks before writing to etcd. Skipping this mental model causes confusion about why a valid YAML is rejected or why changes don't take effect immediately.

children.title: Pod Phases and Conditions
children.description: Pods move through Pending, Running, Succeeded, Failed, and Unknown phases. Misreading these phases — especially confusing Pending with scheduling failures — wastes hours of debugging time and leads to wrong fixes.

children.title: Liveness and Readiness Probes
children.description: Liveness probes restart stuck containers; readiness probes gate traffic routing. Misconfiguring them — too aggressive thresholds or wrong endpoints — causes cascading restarts or leaves broken pods in the load balancer rotation.

children.title: ReplicaSet vs Deployment
children.description: Deployments own ReplicaSets and manage rollout strategy; you almost never create a ReplicaSet directly. Confusing the two leads to manual scaling fights between the Deployment controller and hand-edited ReplicaSets.

children.title: Rolling Updates and Rollbacks
children.description: Deployments support RollingUpdate and Recreate strategies. A poorly configured maxUnavailable or maxSurge causes downtime during updates; not knowing how to rollback means a bad deploy stays live while you rebuild the image.

children.title: Label Selector Semantics
children.description: Services and ReplicaSets select pods purely by labels; the pod template's labels must match the selector exactly. A mismatch means your Service sends traffic nowhere, which is one of the top causes of zero-response debugging sessions.

children.title: Namespaces and Resource Isolation
children.description: Namespaces provide soft multi-tenancy — they scope names, RBAC, and quotas but do not isolate network traffic by default. Assuming namespace isolation is complete without NetworkPolicies is a common security misconfiguration.

kubernetes.title: Configuration and Storage
kubernetes.description: This layer covers managing application configuration and persistent data. It matters because real applications need configuration, secrets, and durable storage.
kubernetes.challenge: Run a stateful database in the cluster using a StatefulSet with persistent storage and Secrets.

children.title: ConfigMaps — Environment and Volume Injection
children.description: ConfigMaps decouple runtime configuration from container images and can be mounted as files or injected as environment variables. Injecting as env vars makes the value immutable until pod restart; mounting as a volume allows live updates — picking the wrong method causes hard-to-debug stale config.

children.title: Secrets and Secure Handling
children.description: Secrets store sensitive values that Kubernetes base64-encodes but does not encrypt at rest by default. Without enabling etcd encryption or an external secrets manager, any cluster admin can read all Secret values — a critical security gap in production clusters.

children.title: PersistentVolumes and Claims
children.description: PersistentVolumeClaims decouple pod storage requests from the underlying provisioner. Misunderstanding the binding lifecycle — especially that a released PV is not immediately available — causes StatefulSet pods to stay Pending indefinitely.

children.title: Storage Classes and Dynamic Provisioning
children.description: StorageClasses let the cluster provision volumes on demand from cloud providers or CSI drivers. Without a default StorageClass, unqualified PVCs remain Pending forever, and teams waste time wondering why their StatefulSet never starts.

children.title: Ordered Pod Identity
children.description: StatefulSets assign stable, ordered names and persistent volume claims to each pod replica. Databases rely on this to elect a primary by pod index; without it a restarted replica loses its identity and cannot reconnect to its data.

children.title: Headless Services for StatefulSets
children.description: A headless Service (clusterIP: None) gives each StatefulSet pod a stable DNS A record so peers can address one another directly. Without it, clients hit a random pod through the normal ClusterIP, which breaks leader-election protocols.

children.title: Init Containers
children.description: Init containers run sequentially before app containers start and share the pod's volumes. They handle database migration or secret fetching in a predictable order; without them developers bolt migration logic into app startup code where failures are harder to isolate.

children.title: Sidecar Pattern
children.description: Sidecars run alongside the main container in the same pod and share its network and volume mounts. Service meshes like Istio and logging agents like Fluentd depend on this pattern; misunderstanding pod-level resource sharing leads to resource accounting errors.

kubernetes.title: Packaging with Helm
kubernetes.description: This layer teaches templating and packaging Kubernetes applications with Helm. It matters because Helm makes complex deployments repeatable and configurable.
kubernetes.challenge: Package your application as a Helm chart with configurable values for multiple environments.

children.title: Template Functions and Flow Control
children.description: Helm templates use Go's text/template engine with Sprig helpers. Without mastering range, if/else, and functions like tpl and include, charts become rigid — every environment variation requires a hard-coded value file instead of a composable template.

children.title: Named Templates and Partials
children.description: Named templates (define/include) let you DRY up repeated label blocks and container specs across chart templates. Ignoring them leads to copy-pasted YAML that diverges silently across releases, causing label mismatches that break Services.

children.title: Upgrades, Rollbacks, and History
children.description: Helm stores each release revision in a Secret and supports atomic upgrades with automatic rollback on hook failure. Not using --atomic in production means a failed upgrade leaves the cluster in a half-upgraded state with no automatic recovery.

children.title: Chart Dependencies and Subcharts
children.description: Helm dependencies pull in upstream charts (databases, caches) as subcharts versioned in Chart.yaml. Without this, teams vendor raw manifests manually and lose upstream security patches because there is no automated upgrade path.

children.title: OCI Registries for Charts
children.description: Helm 3 supports pushing charts to OCI registries like Docker Hub or GitHub Container Registry alongside images. Not using a registry means distributing charts as tarballs over shared drives, which breaks versioning and access control.

children.title: Helm Hooks
children.description: Hooks run Jobs at specific points in the release lifecycle — pre-install, post-upgrade, pre-delete. Without hooks, database migrations either run inside the app (risking race conditions) or require manual out-of-band steps that are forgotten.

children.title: helm lint and helm template
children.description: helm lint catches structural errors in a chart before deploy; helm template renders manifests locally so you can diff against the running state. Skipping these means syntax bugs reach the cluster and produce cryptic API server errors.

children.title: Chart Testing with ct
children.description: The chart-testing (ct) tool installs a chart into a real cluster and runs helm test to validate it. Without this step, charts work in development but fail in CI because hidden dependencies on cluster state are never exposed.

kubernetes.title: Networking and Ingress
kubernetes.description: This layer explores how traffic flows in and across a cluster. It matters because exposing and securing services correctly is central to running real workloads.
kubernetes.challenge: Expose multiple services through an Ingress controller with TLS and path-based routing.

children.title: CNI Plugins and the Flat Network
children.description: Kubernetes requires every pod to have a routable IP reachable from all other pods without NAT. The CNI plugin (Calico, Cilium, Flannel) implements this guarantee; choosing the wrong plugin or misconfiguring it causes intermittent pod-to-pod failures that are extremely hard to reproduce.

children.title: kube-proxy and iptables
children.description: kube-proxy programs iptables rules to load-balance traffic hitting a Service ClusterIP across its backing pods. When rules become stale after rapid pod churn, connections silently fail; understanding this layer tells you where to look when Services are reachable but requests fail.

children.title: Ingress Controllers
children.description: An IngressClass ties Ingress resources to a specific controller (NGINX, Traefik, etc.). Without specifying the class, multiple controllers fight over the same Ingress, causing non-deterministic routing failures.

children.title: Gateway API
children.description: Gateway API is the next-generation successor to Ingress with richer routing primitives and RBAC-friendly role separation between infrastructure operators and app developers. Teams ignoring it today will face a migration when Ingress is eventually deprecated.

children.title: Default-Deny and Allowlist Model
children.description: By default all pods accept traffic from all other pods. A single default-deny NetworkPolicy flips this to an explicit allowlist model; without it a compromised pod can reach any database in the cluster regardless of namespace boundaries.

children.title: DNS and Service Discovery
children.description: CoreDNS resolves service names to ClusterIPs using the pattern service.namespace.svc.cluster.local. Hardcoding IPs instead of DNS names means every pod restart breaks dependent services; understanding the FQDN structure prevents a whole class of connectivity bugs.

children.title: cert-manager and Certificate Lifecycle
children.description: cert-manager automates TLS certificate issuance and renewal via Let's Encrypt or internal CAs. Without it, engineers manually track certificate expiry, and expired certs cause immediate outages — often at inconvenient hours.

children.title: TLS Termination at the Ingress
children.description: Terminating TLS at the Ingress keeps backend services simple and centralises certificate management. Terminating too early (before the load balancer) or too late (at each pod) creates either unencrypted internal hops or certificate sprawl across hundreds of pods.

kubernetes.title: Service Mesh with Istio
kubernetes.description: This layer introduces advanced traffic management and security with a service mesh. It matters because meshes provide observability, mTLS, and traffic control without code changes.
kubernetes.challenge: Install Istio and use traffic splitting to roll out a canary version of one of your services.

children.title: Sidecar Proxy Injection
children.description: Istio injects an Envoy sidecar into every pod via a MutatingWebhook, intercepting all inbound and outbound traffic transparently. If the webhook is misconfigured or a namespace lacks the injection label, pods bypass the mesh and lose mTLS, telemetry, and policy enforcement silently.

children.title: VirtualService and DestinationRule
children.description: VirtualService defines routing rules for HTTP/gRPC traffic; DestinationRule configures load-balancing and circuit-breaking per destination. Applying one without the other — routing to a subset without defining that subset in a DestinationRule — results in 503 errors with no obvious cause.

children.title: Mutual TLS (mTLS) Modes
children.description: Istio operates in PERMISSIVE mode (accepts plain and mTLS) or STRICT mode (mTLS only). Running PERMISSIVE in production provides no actual encryption guarantee — any pod can send plaintext; enabling STRICT without checking that all services are mesh-enrolled breaks them instantly.

children.title: AuthorizationPolicy
children.description: AuthorizationPolicy enforces which services may call which endpoints based on source principal and request attributes. Without it the mesh provides encryption but no access control — every service can still reach every other service.

children.title: Retries and Timeouts
children.description: Istio can inject configurable retries and per-request timeouts at the proxy layer without changing application code. Ignoring this means every team reimplements retry logic inconsistently in each service, leading to retry storms when a downstream service degrades.

children.title: Circuit Breaking with Envoy
children.description: Envoy's circuit breaker ejects unhealthy endpoints from the load-balancing pool before they cascade failures to callers. Without it, a single slow pod receives the same traffic as healthy ones, multiplying latency tail across the entire call chain.

children.title: Distributed Tracing via Envoy
children.description: Istio propagates trace context headers automatically through Envoy, but application code must forward them between calls. Missing this propagation creates disconnected traces that make it impossible to correlate latency across service boundaries.

children.title: Kiali Service Graph
children.description: Kiali visualises the mesh topology and real-time traffic health derived from Prometheus metrics. Without it, operators debug service dependencies by reading YAML instead of seeing live traffic flow, massively increasing incident resolution time.

kubernetes.title: Security and RBAC
kubernetes.description: This layer covers securing clusters and workloads. It matters because misconfigured permissions and pods are a leading cause of cluster breaches.
kubernetes.challenge: Lock down a namespace with RBAC, pod security standards, and an admission policy that blocks risky pods.

children.title: Roles and ClusterRoles
children.description: Roles grant permissions within a single namespace; ClusterRoles can be bound cluster-wide or to a namespace. Conflating the two — giving a developer a ClusterRole when a Role suffices — violates least privilege and grants read access to Secrets in every namespace.

children.title: Service Account Tokens
children.description: Every pod gets a mounted service account token that Kubernetes and cloud IAM (via IRSA, Workload Identity) use for API calls. Leaving the default service account with broad cluster permissions means any compromised pod can enumerate and modify cluster resources.

children.title: Pod Security Standards
children.description: Kubernetes defines three built-in policy levels — Privileged, Baseline, Restricted — enforced via the Pod Security Admission controller. Without enforcing at least Baseline, any developer can schedule a privileged pod that mounts the host filesystem and escapes the container.

children.title: SecurityContext Fields
children.description: SecurityContext on the pod and container level controls runAsUser, allowPrivilegeEscalation, readOnlyRootFilesystem, and capabilities. Each field narrows the blast radius of a container escape; leaving them at defaults means containers run as root with full Linux capabilities.

children.title: OPA Gatekeeper Policies
children.description: Gatekeeper evaluates OPA Rego policies as a ValidatingWebhook, rejecting non-compliant resources before they reach etcd. Without policy guardrails, human error — misconfigured resources, missing labels, unlabelled images — reaches production undetected.

children.title: Kyverno Policy Engine
children.description: Kyverno writes Kubernetes-native policies using familiar YAML instead of Rego, covering validation, mutation, and generation in one tool. Teams avoiding Rego's learning curve often choose Kyverno to enforce the same guardrails with faster onboarding.

children.title: External Secrets Operator
children.description: External Secrets Operator syncs secrets from AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, or HashiCorp Vault into Kubernetes Secrets automatically. Without it, teams manually create Kubernetes Secrets from external stores, and rotated values require manual pod restarts.

children.title: Vault Agent Injector
children.description: The Vault Agent Injector injects a sidecar that retrieves and renews secrets from Vault at pod start and during rotation. Compared to static Kubernetes Secrets, it provides short-lived credentials and audit logging for every access, eliminating long-lived secret sprawl.

kubernetes.title: Observability and Autoscaling
kubernetes.description: This layer teaches monitoring clusters and scaling workloads automatically. It matters because clusters must adapt to load while remaining observable and cost-efficient.
kubernetes.challenge: Add resource limits, a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, and Prometheus monitoring to your workloads.

children.title: Requests and Limits
children.description: CPU requests determine scheduling placement on a node; memory limits set the OOM kill ceiling. Setting no requests allows the scheduler to pack too many pods onto a node, causing evictions under pressure; setting limits without requests makes throttling unpredictable.

children.title: Quality of Service Classes
children.description: Kubernetes assigns Guaranteed, Burstable, or BestEffort QoS to pods based on their requests/limits configuration. BestEffort pods are evicted first under memory pressure; not knowing this causes critical services to be evicted when their QoS class was never intentionally configured.

children.title: HPA v2 and Custom Metrics
children.description: HPA v2 scales workloads on CPU, memory, or any metric exposed via the custom or external metrics API. Relying only on CPU can under-scale a latency-sensitive service during I/O bursts; custom metrics from Prometheus allow scaling on request rate or queue depth instead.

children.title: KEDA — Event-Driven Autoscaling
children.description: KEDA scales workloads to zero and back up based on event sources — SQS depth, Kafka lag, Cron — that the built-in HPA cannot express. Without KEDA, teams either over-provision idle consumers or write custom operators to achieve the same behaviour.

children.title: Vertical Pod Autoscaler
children.description: VPA right-sizes CPU and memory requests by observing historical usage and recommending or automatically applying new values. Without VPA, request values are guessed at deploy time and never revisited, causing perpetual over-provisioning or recurring OOM kills.

children.title: Cluster Autoscaler and Karpenter
children.description: Cluster Autoscaler adds or removes nodes when pods are unschedulable or nodes are underutilised. Karpenter replaces it with a faster, topology-aware provisioner that picks the cheapest instance type per workload. Ignoring node-level autoscaling forces manual capacity planning that lags demand.

children.title: kube-prometheus-stack
children.description: The kube-prometheus-stack Helm chart deploys Prometheus, Grafana, Alertmanager, and a set of pre-built dashboards and rules in one operation. Building this manually from scratch takes days and produces a non-standard configuration that diverges from community best practices.

children.title: ServiceMonitor and PodMonitor
children.description: Prometheus Operator uses ServiceMonitor and PodMonitor CRDs to declare which targets to scrape without editing a static prometheus.yml. Without them, adding a new service requires a cluster-admin to manually edit the Prometheus configuration and reload it.

kubernetes.title: GitOps and Cluster Delivery
kubernetes.description: This layer covers deploying to clusters declaratively from Git. It matters because GitOps makes cluster changes auditable, repeatable, and self-healing.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitOps with ArgoCD

kubernetes.challenge: Deliver your Helm-packaged application to the cluster through ArgoCD synced from Git.

children.title: Declarative Desired State in Git
children.description: GitOps treats a Git repository as the single source of truth for cluster state; any drift from that state is detected and corrected automatically. Without this, cluster state lives in operator memory and CI pipelines — it diverges silently and becomes unrecoverable after personnel changes.

children.title: ArgoCD Application Model
children.description: ArgoCD Applications reference a Git source and a destination cluster/namespace, then sync the diff on a schedule or webhook trigger. Not understanding sync waves and resource hooks causes database migrations to run after the app deploys, producing startup failures on every release.

children.title: Argo Rollouts — Canary and Blue/Green
children.description: Argo Rollouts extends Kubernetes Deployments with canary and blue/green strategies gated by analysis runs. Without progressive delivery, every release is an all-or-nothing risk — a single bad deploy affects all users before anyone can react.

children.title: Analysis Templates and Metric Gates
children.description: AnalysisTemplates define success criteria — error rate below threshold, latency p99 under limit — that Argo Rollouts queries before promoting a canary. Without automated gates, promotion is a manual judgement call that is frequently skipped under release pressure.

children.title: ArgoCD ApplicationSets
children.description: ApplicationSets generate ArgoCD Applications dynamically from templates and generators — cluster lists, Git directories, or matrix combinations. Without them, managing 20 clusters means maintaining 20 hand-edited Application manifests that drift from each other over time.

children.title: Fleet and Hub-Spoke Topologies
children.description: A hub ArgoCD instance manages spoke clusters by storing their kubeconfig credentials as ArgoCD cluster secrets. Not planning the hub-spoke topology before scaling to multiple clusters forces a painful migration later, during which clusters may lose sync guarantees.

children.title: Argo CD Image Updater
children.description: Argo CD Image Updater watches container registries and commits new image tags back to Git, closing the loop between CI and GitOps without manual tag updates. Without it, developers either hardcode image tags in Git or rely on mutable tags like latest, which breaks rollback auditability.

children.title: Flux Image Automation
children.description: Flux's image-automation controller scans registries and opens Git commits or PRs with updated image references, providing an audited change history for every image promotion. Teams using Flux instead of ArgoCD rely on this for continuous delivery without touching CI pipelines.

kubernetes.title: Production Cluster Operations
kubernetes.description: This layer covers operating clusters reliably at production scale. It matters because production clusters must stay available, upgradeable, and recoverable under real traffic.
kubernetes.challenge: Operate a production-grade cluster with backups, an upgrade plan, quotas, and a documented recovery runbook.

children.title: Cluster Upgrades — Control Plane First
children.description: Kubernetes supports upgrading one minor version at a time; the control plane must be upgraded before nodes. Skipping a version or upgrading nodes first violates the supported skew policy and can leave the API server unable to communicate with older node kubelets.

children.title: etcd Backup and Restore
children.description: etcd holds every Kubernetes object; a snapshot is the only recovery path after a control-plane failure. Without automated, tested snapshots sent off-cluster, a single etcd data-directory corruption destroys the entire cluster state with no recovery option.

children.title: ResourceQuotas and LimitRanges
children.description: ResourceQuotas cap total CPU, memory, and object counts per namespace; LimitRanges enforce default and maximum values per container. Without them a single misconfigured Deployment can exhaust all node capacity, starving every other team's workloads.

children.title: Hierarchical Namespaces
children.description: Hierarchical Namespace Controller (HNC) lets teams own a tree of namespaces with inherited RBAC and policies. Without HNC, platform teams manually duplicate RoleBindings across dozens of team namespaces, and any update must be applied N times.

children.title: Velero for Cluster Backup
children.description: Velero backs up Kubernetes resources and PersistentVolume snapshots to object storage and restores them to the same or a different cluster. Without it, recovering from a misconfigured mass-delete requires rebuilding manifests from Git plus manually restoring all stateful data.

children.title: PodDisruptionBudgets
children.description: PodDisruptionBudgets guarantee a minimum number of pods stay available during voluntary disruptions like node drains or upgrades. Without them, a rolling node upgrade can take down all replicas of a service simultaneously, causing a maintenance-window outage.

children.title: kubectl debug and Ephemeral Containers
children.description: Ephemeral containers let you attach a debug sidecar to a running pod without restarting it — critical when the pod image lacks shell tools. Not knowing this forces teams to either bake debug tools into production images (bloat and security risk) or redeploy with a modified image (loses the failing state).

children.title: Node Pressure and Eviction Thresholds
children.description: The kubelet evicts pods when a node exceeds memory or disk pressure thresholds, following QoS class order. Without understanding eviction thresholds, teams misattribute pod restarts to application bugs, spending days adding retry logic instead of adjusting requests or adding nodes.

aws-dev.title: AWS Cloud Fundamentals
aws-dev.description: This layer introduces the AWS global infrastructure and core account concepts. It matters because regions, identity, and networking underpin every service you will use.
aws-dev.challenge: Create an IAM user with least-privilege policies and access AWS through both the console and CLI.

children.title: IAM Policies and Least Privilege
children.description: IAM policies define exactly what actions a principal can perform on which resources. Without least-privilege scoping, a compromised credential can cascade into a full account breach — S3 data exfiltration, EC2 manipulation, and billing abuse all become trivial.

children.title: IAM Roles vs. Users
children.description: Roles provide temporary credentials assumed by services, users, or external identities — eliminating long-lived access keys. Misusing static keys instead of roles is the single most common cause of AWS credential leaks in CI/CD pipelines and container workloads.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Replace IAM Users with Roles

children.title: Regions and Availability Zones
children.description: AWS Regions are geographically isolated, each containing multiple Availability Zones connected by low-latency links. Deploying across a single AZ without understanding this model means a single data-center failure can take your service offline entirely.

children.title: Shared Responsibility Model
