children.description: AWS secures the cloud infrastructure itself; you are responsible for securing everything you deploy within it — OS patches, encryption, IAM, and network configuration. Misunderstanding this boundary leads engineers to skip controls they assume AWS handles.

children.title: AWS CLI Configuration
children.description: The AWS CLI is the programmatic entry point to every service and underpins automation scripts, CI pipelines, and local development. A misconfigured profile pointing at the wrong region or account silently operates on unintended infrastructure.

children.title: Billing, Free Tier, and Cost Alerts
children.description: AWS bills by consumption — unused resources like idle EC2 instances, forgotten NAT Gateways, or undeleted EBS snapshots accumulate charges silently. Setting billing alarms and understanding the Free Tier boundary prevents unexpected bills during learning and development.

children.title: VPC Fundamentals
children.description: A VPC is your private network inside AWS where all resources live. Without understanding CIDR blocks, subnets, and route tables you cannot reason about why traffic reaches or fails to reach your services.

children.title: AWS Console Navigation
children.description: The AWS Management Console exposes hundreds of services and sub-services; knowing how to navigate resource pages, read service quotas, and interpret service health prevents wasted time and misconfigured deployments.

resources.label: AWS Skill Builder — Cloud Practitioner Essentials

aws-dev.title: Compute with EC2
aws-dev.description: This layer covers provisioning and managing virtual servers on AWS. It matters because EC2 remains a core compute primitive and teaches fundamental cloud operations.
aws-dev.challenge: Launch an EC2 instance behind a load balancer in an Auto Scaling group serving a simple web app.

children.title: Instance Types and Pricing Models
children.description: EC2 offers dozens of instance families optimized for compute, memory, storage, or GPU workloads. Picking the wrong family wastes money or creates bottlenecks; misunderstanding On-Demand vs. Spot vs. Reserved pricing can mean paying three times more than necessary.

children.title: AMIs and User Data Scripts
children.description: AMIs capture the OS, pre-installed software, and configuration for your instances. User data scripts run at launch to automate bootstrapping — without them, every instance requires manual setup, making Auto Scaling groups and immutable infrastructure impossible.

children.title: Auto Scaling Groups
children.description: Auto Scaling Groups automatically adjust the number of EC2 instances based on demand metrics or schedules. Without them, traffic spikes cause outages and idle capacity wastes money — the two most common EC2 operational failures in production.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Auto Scaling Best Practices

children.title: Elastic Load Balancing
children.description: ELB distributes incoming traffic across multiple EC2 instances and integrates with Auto Scaling to drain unhealthy targets. Skipping load balancers means a single instance crash takes down your entire service instead of routing traffic to healthy peers.

children.title: EBS Volumes and Snapshots
children.description: EBS provides durable block storage attached to EC2 instances — essential for databases, application data, and OS volumes. Without regular EBS snapshots you have no recovery point if an instance or volume is terminated, making data loss permanent.

children.title: Security Groups and Key Pairs
children.description: Security groups act as virtual firewalls controlling inbound and outbound traffic per instance. Over-permissive rules (0.0.0.0/0 on port 22) are the leading cause of EC2 intrusions — understanding stateful rule evaluation prevents this category of mistake entirely.

children.title: Instance Lifecycle and State Management
children.description: EC2 instances transition through pending, running, stopping, stopped, and terminated states with distinct billing and data implications. Confusing stopped (data preserved) with terminated (EBS deleted) leads to irreversible data loss in production.

children.title: EC2 Instance Connect and SSM Session Manager
children.description: SSM Session Manager allows shell access to instances without opening SSH ports or managing key pairs, reducing the attack surface dramatically. Relying on bastion hosts and open port 22 instead is a security anti-pattern that most modern AWS environments have eliminated.

aws-dev.title: Storage with S3
aws-dev.description: This layer teaches object storage and content delivery on AWS. It matters because S3 is the backbone of storage, backups, and static hosting in the AWS ecosystem.
aws-dev.challenge: Host a static site on S3 with versioning, lifecycle rules, and a CloudFront distribution in front.

children.title: Buckets, Objects, and Permissions
children.description: S3 stores objects in buckets with a flat namespace; access is controlled through bucket policies, ACLs, and IAM. A publicly accessible bucket due to a misconfigured policy is one of the most common causes of large-scale data breaches in AWS environments.

children.title: Storage Classes and Lifecycle Rules
children.description: S3 offers storage classes from Standard to Glacier Deep Archive with orders-of-magnitude cost differences. Without lifecycle rules, data accumulates in expensive Standard storage indefinitely — a common cause of runaway S3 costs in production accounts.

children.title: S3 Versioning and Encryption
children.description: Versioning preserves every version of every object, enabling recovery from accidental deletes and overwrites. Without it, a single erroneous delete or application bug can permanently destroy data that took months to accumulate.

children.title: Cross-Region Replication
children.description: S3 Cross-Region Replication asynchronously copies objects to a bucket in a different region, supporting disaster recovery and data residency requirements. Without it, a regional outage can make all your object data unavailable for the duration of the event.

children.title: S3 Static Website Hosting
children.description: S3 can serve HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and assets directly as a static website at very low cost and without managing servers. Skipping the index and error document configuration causes navigation and 404 handling to break for single-page applications.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Host a Static Website

children.title: CloudFront CDN Distribution
children.description: CloudFront caches S3 content at edge locations worldwide, reducing latency and S3 request costs. Without a CDN, every user request hits the origin bucket directly — increasing latency for global users and producing unexpectedly high per-request charges at scale.

children.title: Pre-signed URLs and Access Points
children.description: Pre-signed URLs grant time-limited access to specific S3 objects without making the bucket public or sharing credentials. Without them, teams resort to making buckets public or over-sharing IAM credentials — both serious security anti-patterns.

children.title: S3 Event Notifications
children.description: S3 can trigger Lambda functions, SQS queues, or SNS topics on object events like PUT and DELETE, enabling event-driven pipelines. Without this pattern, applications must poll S3 for changes — creating latency, wasted API calls, and increased complexity.

resources.label: AWS Blog: S3 and Lambda Integration

aws-dev.title: Serverless with Lambda
aws-dev.description: This layer covers building event-driven serverless applications. It matters because serverless reduces operational overhead and is a key modern AWS pattern.
aws-dev.challenge: Build a serverless REST API using Lambda, API Gateway, and DynamoDB for a small data service.

children.title: Function Runtimes and Execution Model
children.description: Lambda runs code in isolated execution environments, billing only for the duration your code runs in 1ms increments. Misunderstanding the ephemeral execution model — expecting persistent state in memory or disk across invocations — causes silent data loss and hard-to-reproduce bugs.

children.title: Event Sources and Triggers
children.description: Lambda integrates natively with dozens of AWS services as event sources — S3, SQS, API Gateway, DynamoDB Streams, and more. Misconfiguring event source mappings, especially batch sizes and error handling on SQS, leads to message loss or infinite retry loops.

children.title: API Gateway Integration
children.description: API Gateway fronts Lambda functions with HTTP endpoints, handling auth, rate limiting, and request/response transformation. Without understanding proxy vs. non-proxy integrations, response formats are silently mangled and debugging requires reproducing conditions in production.

children.title: Cold Starts and Performance Tuning
children.description: Lambda cold starts add latency when a new execution environment is initialized — critical for user-facing APIs. Without provisioned concurrency or optimized package sizes, latency spikes under low traffic or after deployments degrade user experience unpredictably.

children.title: DynamoDB Data Modeling and Keys
children.description: DynamoDB performance is determined almost entirely by partition key design — a hot partition from a poorly chosen key causes throttling that no amount of provisioned capacity can fix. Getting the access pattern model right before writing a single item is non-negotiable.

children.title: DynamoDB Streams and TTL
children.description: DynamoDB Streams capture a time-ordered sequence of item changes, enabling event-driven architectures and cross-region replication. TTL automatically deletes expired items at no cost — ignoring it means stale data accumulates forever, increasing storage costs and query noise.

children.title: Lambda Execution Roles and Permissions
children.description: Lambda functions must have IAM execution roles granting only the permissions they need. Over-permissioned roles — attaching AdministratorAccess to a Lambda — mean a single injection vulnerability grants an attacker full account control.

children.title: Lambda Layers and Deployment Packages
children.description: Lambda Layers share libraries and dependencies across multiple functions without bundling them into each deployment package. Without layers, repeated library uploads consume storage limits, slow deployments, and make dependency updates error-prone across dozens of functions.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Lambda Layers Best Practices

aws-dev.title: Networking and Security
aws-dev.description: This layer deepens VPC networking and access control. It matters because secure, well-segmented networks are required for any production AWS workload.
aws-dev.challenge: Design a VPC with public and private subnets and place your app and database in the correct tiers.

children.title: Subnets, Route Tables, and Gateways
children.description: Public subnets route through an Internet Gateway; private subnets use a NAT Gateway or remain isolated. Placing databases or backend services in public subnets — a common beginner mistake — exposes them directly to the internet.

children.title: VPC Peering and Private Endpoints
children.description: VPC Endpoints route traffic to AWS services (S3, DynamoDB, etc.) over the private network without traversing the internet, eliminating NAT Gateway data-transfer charges and network exposure. Without them, all service traffic leaves the VPC — adding latency and cost.

children.title: Security Groups vs. NACLs
children.description: Security groups are stateful per-resource firewalls; NACLs are stateless per-subnet filters evaluated in rule-number order. Confusing the two leads to rules that work in one direction but silently block return traffic, causing intermittent connection failures.

children.title: IAM Policy Evaluation Logic
children.description: AWS evaluates IAM using an explicit deny-first model — a Deny anywhere overrides any Allow. Misunderstanding evaluation order causes engineers to add Allow policies that have no effect because a service control policy or boundary is silently denying the action.

children.title: AWS Secrets Manager
children.description: Secrets Manager stores, rotates, and audits credentials — database passwords, API keys, and OAuth tokens — with automatic rotation for RDS. Hardcoding secrets in code or environment variables is the most common source of credential exposure in production applications.

children.title: AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store
children.description: Parameter Store provides hierarchical, versioned configuration storage — free for standard parameters — that integrates with IAM for per-parameter access control. Without it, teams share config through unencrypted environment variables or S3 files with overly broad access.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Parameter Store vs. Secrets Manager

children.title: VPC Flow Logs
children.description: VPC Flow Logs record metadata about accepted and rejected traffic — essential for security investigations and debugging connectivity issues. Without them, you have no audit trail of who communicated with what inside your network.

children.title: AWS WAF and Shield
children.description: WAF filters HTTP traffic with managed and custom rules protecting against SQL injection, XSS, and rate-based attacks. Exposing an application to the internet without WAF means your endpoints are scanned and probed constantly with no automated protection layer.

aws-dev.title: Infrastructure as Code with CloudFormation
aws-dev.description: This layer teaches declaring AWS infrastructure as templates. It matters because reproducible, version-controlled infrastructure is essential for reliable AWS environments.
aws-dev.challenge: Reprovision your EC2, S3, and networking resources entirely through a CloudFormation template.

children.title: Template Structure and Change Sets
children.description: CloudFormation templates declare AWS resources as YAML or JSON; change sets preview the delta before execution. Applying a stack update without reviewing the change set can replace stateful resources like RDS instances with zero warning, causing data loss.

children.title: Nested Stacks and Cross-Stack References
children.description: Nested stacks decompose large templates into manageable modules; cross-stack references share outputs between independent stacks. Without this modularization, a single monolithic template becomes impossible to test, review, or update without full-stack replacement risk.

children.title: CDK Constructs and Stacks
children.description: The AWS CDK lets you define cloud infrastructure in TypeScript, Python, Java, or Go using familiar programming abstractions — loops, conditionals, and reusable classes. Without understanding L1, L2, and L3 construct levels you either write verbose low-level code or miss the opinionated defaults that L2 constructs enforce.

children.title: CDK Pipelines and cdk deploy
children.description: CDK Pipelines is a high-level construct that self-mutates a CodePipeline to continuously deploy your CDK app — including the pipeline itself. Without it, infrastructure changes require manual cdk deploy steps that break the repeatability guarantees of infrastructure as code.

children.title: Drift Detection and Stack Policies
children.description: CloudFormation drift detection compares deployed resources against the template definition, surfacing manual console changes that break template integrity. Without it, your infrastructure slowly diverges from code until the next stack update produces unexpected replacements.

children.title: CloudFormation Rollback and Deletion Policies
children.description: Deletion policies control whether resources are retained, snapshotted, or destroyed when a stack is deleted. Without setting Retain or Snapshot on databases and S3 buckets, a single cdk destroy or stack delete permanently destroys production data.

children.title: CloudFormation Parameters and SSM Integration
children.description: Template parameters allow values to be supplied at deploy time; SSM Parameter Store integration resolves values dynamically without hardcoding AMI IDs or account-specific strings. Hardcoded values make templates non-portable and silently break when AMIs are deprecated.

children.title: CDK Testing with Jest
children.description: CDK applications can be tested with fine-grained assertions and snapshot tests against the synthesized CloudFormation template before any deployment occurs. Without tests, refactoring CDK constructs silently removes security groups, encryption settings, or IAM policies.

aws-dev.title: Databases and Messaging
aws-dev.description: This layer covers managed databases and asynchronous messaging on AWS. It matters because durable data and decoupled communication are core to scalable architectures.
aws-dev.challenge: Add an RDS database and an SQS queue to decouple a background worker in your serverless API.

children.title: Metrics, Dimensions, and Namespaces
children.description: CloudWatch organizes metrics by namespace and dimensions — without understanding this structure you cannot query service-specific data or distinguish metrics from different resources of the same type. Misread metrics cause incorrect alarms that fire too often or miss real incidents.

children.title: CloudWatch Logs and Log Insights
children.description: CloudWatch Logs aggregates structured and unstructured logs from Lambda, EC2, ECS, and other services; Log Insights queries them with a SQL-like language. Without centralized logs, debugging production failures requires SSH access to individual instances or grepping distributed log files.

children.title: AWS X-Ray Tracing
children.description: X-Ray traces requests end-to-end across Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, and downstream services — producing service maps and latency percentiles. Without tracing, a slow request appears as a single high-latency data point with no visibility into which downstream call caused it.

children.title: CloudTrail Audit Logging
children.description: CloudTrail records every API call made in your AWS account — who called what, from where, and when. Without it, a security incident becomes impossible to investigate because there is no record of what actions were taken or which credentials were used.

children.title: CloudWatch Dashboards
children.description: CloudWatch Dashboards provide a single-pane view of metrics across multiple services and accounts. Without dashboards, operators must navigate dozens of individual service consoles during an incident — dramatically increasing mean time to diagnosis.

children.title: SNS and Alarm Notifications
children.description: CloudWatch Alarms integrate with SNS to deliver notifications to email, Slack, PagerDuty, or Lambda when thresholds are breached. Without alarm routing, engineers discover incidents from user complaints rather than automated detection.

children.title: AWS Config and Compliance Rules
children.description: AWS Config continuously records configuration changes and evaluates resources against compliance rules — detecting drift like unencrypted S3 buckets or open security groups as soon as they appear. Without it, security misconfigurations can persist for months undetected.

children.title: EventBridge for Operational Automation
children.description: EventBridge routes events from AWS services, custom applications, and SaaS to targets like Lambda or Step Functions, enabling automated remediation. Without event-driven automation, operational responses require human intervention for tasks that could be resolved in seconds.

aws-dev.title: Observability and Operations
aws-dev.description: This layer teaches monitoring, logging, and tracing AWS workloads. It matters because visibility into systems is required to operate them reliably.
aws-dev.challenge: Add CloudWatch alarms, dashboards, and X-Ray tracing to your application and route alerts to email.

children.title: Amazon RDS Multi-AZ and Read Replicas
children.description: RDS Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby in a different AZ and fails over automatically in 60-120 seconds. Without it, a single AZ failure takes your database offline and requires manual recovery — violating typical production SLA requirements.

children.title: RDS Backups and Parameter Groups
children.description: Automated backups enable point-in-time recovery; parameter groups tune database engine settings. Without configured backups and a tested restore procedure, data loss from corruption or accidental deletion becomes permanent.

children.title: Amazon SQS and Message Queuing
children.description: SQS decouples producers and consumers, absorbing traffic bursts and preventing downstream services from being overwhelmed. Without queues, a traffic spike that exhausts one service propagates failures upstream, turning a localized problem into a system-wide outage.

children.title: Amazon SNS Fan-Out Pattern
children.description: SNS delivers messages to multiple SQS queues, Lambda functions, or HTTP endpoints simultaneously from a single publish — the fan-out pattern. Without it, publishers must maintain a list of downstream consumers and call each one individually, creating tight coupling.

children.title: ElastiCache for Redis and Memcached
children.description: ElastiCache provides managed Redis and Memcached clusters for session storage, database query caching, and rate limiting. Without a caching layer, every request hits the database — eventually saturating connection pools and causing cascading failures under load.

children.title: DAX — DynamoDB Accelerator
children.description: DAX is a fully managed, in-memory cache for DynamoDB that delivers microsecond read performance without application-side cache logic. Without it, high-volume DynamoDB read workloads exhaust read capacity units and require expensive provisioned throughput increases.

resources.label: AWS Blog: When to Use DAX vs. ElastiCache

children.title: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
children.description: Kinesis ingests real-time streaming data from thousands of sources and makes it available for processing within seconds. Without a streaming solution, event data must be written to a database on every request — creating write bottlenecks and blocking analytics.

resources.label: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams Developer Guide

children.title: AWS Step Functions for Workflows
children.description: Step Functions orchestrates multi-step Lambda workflows with built-in retries, error handling, and parallel branches as a visual state machine. Without it, complex multi-step processes are wired together with fragile manual Lambda-to-Lambda calls and ad-hoc error handling.

aws-dev.title: CI/CD on AWS
aws-dev.description: This layer covers automating builds and deployments using AWS native tooling. It matters because automated delivery is what makes cloud iteration fast and safe.
aws-dev.challenge: Build a CodePipeline that tests and deploys your CloudFormation stack on every commit.

children.title: Composite Alarms and Anomaly Detection
children.description: Composite alarms combine multiple alarms with AND/OR logic to reduce alert noise; anomaly detection uses ML baselines to fire on unusual patterns rather than static thresholds. Without these, teams either miss genuine incidents (thresholds too high) or experience alarm fatigue (thresholds too low).

children.title: CloudWatch Container Insights
children.description: Container Insights collects CPU, memory, disk, and network metrics from ECS and EKS clusters at the task and pod level. Without it, cluster operators see only coarse host-level metrics and cannot pinpoint which container or service is consuming resources.

children.title: Structured JSON Logging for Lambda
children.description: Emitting structured JSON logs with consistent fields like requestId, userId, and level makes Log Insights queries fast and reliable. Unstructured logs require expensive regex parsing and miss fields entirely when log format changes, breaking dashboards silently.

children.title: AWS Distro for OpenTelemetry
children.description: ADOT provides an AWS-supported distribution of OpenTelemetry SDKs and collectors for standardized traces, metrics, and logs across services. Without a standard instrumentation approach, each team instruments differently and trace correlation breaks at service boundaries.

children.title: CodePipeline and CodeBuild
children.description: CodePipeline orchestrates source, build, test, and deploy stages; CodeBuild runs builds in isolated managed containers. Without understanding buildspec.yml and artifact passing between stages, pipelines silently deploy untested artifacts or fail mid-stage with no actionable error.

children.title: CodeDeploy Deployment Strategies
children.description: CodeDeploy automates blue/green, canary, and in-place deployments with automatic rollback on health check failure. Deploying without rollback automation means a failed deployment requires manual intervention while users experience the broken version.

children.title: OIDC Federation for GitHub Actions
children.description: OIDC federation allows GitHub Actions workflows to assume IAM roles without storing AWS credentials as GitHub secrets. Without it, long-lived access keys in secrets become attack surface — if the secret leaks, attackers have persistent AWS access.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Keyless Auth GitHub Actions

children.title: Approval Actions and Manual Gates
children.description: CodePipeline approval actions pause execution and require a human decision before proceeding to production. Without approval gates on production deployments, a merge to main automatically deploys code — eliminating the last human check before user impact.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Release Governance with CodePipeline

aws-dev.title: Production Architecture and Scaling
aws-dev.description: This layer covers running well-architected, scalable workloads in production. It matters because production AWS systems must balance reliability, cost, and security under real demand.
aws-dev.challenge: Architect and deploy a highly available, multi-AZ version of your application reviewed against the Well-Architected pillars.

children.title: Reliability and High Availability Design
children.description: The Well-Architected reliability pillar covers failure modes, recovery objectives, and multi-AZ/multi-region design patterns. Ignoring it means architecture decisions are made without explicit RTO/RPO targets, and failures that should be survivable cause extended outages.

children.title: Security Pillar and AWS Security Hub
children.description: The security pillar mandates identity-based access, data protection, and detection — backed by AWS Security Hub aggregating findings from GuardDuty, Config, and Inspector. Without a unified security posture view, misconfigurations persist undetected across accounts.

children.title: AWS Cost Explorer and Savings Plans
children.description: Cost Explorer visualizes spending by service, account, and tag; Savings Plans commit to usage in exchange for up to 72% discounts. Without visibility into per-resource costs and commitment planning, AWS bills grow unchecked and reserved capacity opportunities are missed.

children.title: Spot Instances and Compute Optimizer
children.description: Spot Instances offer up to 90% savings for interruption-tolerant workloads; Compute Optimizer recommends right-sized instance types based on actual utilization. Skipping both means paying On-Demand prices for oversized instances running batch jobs or stateless services.

children.title: AWS Organizations and SCPs
children.description: AWS Organizations groups accounts into OUs with Service Control Policies that enforce guardrails regardless of individual IAM policies. Without SCPs, a developer account can create resources in prohibited regions or disable CloudTrail — violating compliance requirements.

children.title: AWS Control Tower and Landing Zone
children.description: Control Tower automates multi-account setup with pre-built guardrails, centralized logging, and SSO configuration — a production-ready baseline in hours instead of weeks. Building a landing zone manually without these guardrails leaves accounts non-compliant from day one.

resources.label: AWS Blog: Landing Zone Best Practices

children.title: Disaster Recovery Strategies on AWS
children.description: AWS supports four DR strategies — backup and restore, pilot light, warm standby, and multi-site active/active — each with different RTO, RPO, and cost tradeoffs. Selecting a strategy without modeling these tradeoffs leads to either excessive cost or unmet recovery objectives during actual failures.

children.title: Route 53 Health Checks and Failover
children.description: Route 53 health checks monitor endpoints and automatically route traffic to healthy resources during failures; failover routing policies switch between primary and secondary automatically. Without health-check-based routing, DNS continues pointing users to a failed endpoint indefinitely.

gcp-dev.title: GCP Cloud Fundamentals
gcp-dev.description: This layer introduces the Google Cloud platform structure and identity model. It matters because projects, IAM, and networking are the foundation of every GCP service.
gcp-dev.challenge: Set up a GCP project with IAM roles and access resources through the gcloud CLI.

children.title: Resource Hierarchy
children.description: GCP organizes everything into Organization, Folders, and Projects. Misunderstanding this hierarchy means you cannot apply consistent policies, quotas, or billing boundaries across teams, and IAM bindings end up scattered and unauditable.

children.title: IAM Roles and Policies
children.description: IAM on GCP uses predefined, basic, and custom roles bound to principals at any level of the hierarchy. Granting overly broad roles like Editor at the project level is one of the most common causes of privilege escalation incidents on GCP.

children.title: gcloud CLI
children.description: The gcloud CLI is the primary interface for automating GCP operations. Without mastering gcloud configurations and service account impersonation, you end up relying on the console for tasks that should be scripted and reproducible.

children.title: Billing, Quotas, and Free Tier
children.description: GCP billing is project-scoped and linked to billing accounts; quotas cap resource usage per region. Ignoring quota limits leads to silent failures when services stop accepting requests, and unmonitored spending can result in unexpected bills.

children.title: Regional vs Zonal Resources
children.description: GCP resources are either regional (replicated across zones) or zonal (tied to a single failure domain). Deploying stateful resources in a single zone without replication means a zone outage brings down your entire service.

children.title: VPC Fundamentals on GCP
children.description: GCP VPCs are global by default — a single VPC spans all regions, and subnets are regional. Misunderstanding this auto-mode vs custom-mode distinction leads to unintended network exposure and routing conflicts across regions.

children.title: Service Accounts
children.description: Service accounts are the identity used by GCP workloads to call APIs. Attaching overly permissive service accounts to VMs or Cloud Run services violates least privilege and can allow a compromised container to access every resource in your project.

children.title: Shared Responsibility Model
children.description: GCP manages physical security, hardware, and the hypervisor, but you are responsible for IAM, network configuration, data encryption, and application security. Misunderstanding this boundary leads engineers to assume GCP secures what is actually their own responsibility.

gcp-dev.title: Compute and Cloud Run
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers running workloads from virtual machines to serverless containers. It matters because choosing the right compute option shapes cost and operational effort.
gcp-dev.challenge: Containerize a service and deploy it to Cloud Run with autoscaling enabled.

children.title: Instance Types and Machine Families
children.description: GCP offers general-purpose (N2, E2), compute-optimized (C2), and memory-optimized (M2) machine families. Picking the wrong family wastes money or creates performance bottlenecks — for example, using E2 for CPU-intensive batch jobs that need C2 throughput.

children.title: Managed Instance Groups
children.description: Managed Instance Groups (MIGs) create and manage identically configured VMs with autoscaling and autohealing. Without MIGs, you cannot automatically replace unhealthy instances or scale out under load — making your infrastructure brittle.

children.title: Persistent Disks and Snapshots
children.description: Persistent Disks are network-attached block storage that survive VM deletion; snapshots create point-in-time backups stored in Cloud Storage. Failing to configure scheduled snapshots means a VM disk failure results in permanent data loss.

children.title: Startup Scripts and Metadata
children.description: GCP instance metadata serves startup scripts and custom key-value pairs to VMs at boot time. Without understanding the metadata server, you cannot bootstrap configuration or inject secrets in a repeatable, automation-friendly way.

children.title: Serverless Container Model
children.description: Cloud Run runs stateless containers that scale to zero and back up in milliseconds. Deploying stateful applications without proper session externalization causes data loss when instances scale down, and cold start latency surprises teams that never tested idle behavior.

children.title: Concurrency and Scaling
children.description: Cloud Run uses per-container concurrency (up to 1000 requests per instance by default) and scales based on request load. Misconfiguring concurrency too high for CPU-bound tasks causes latency spikes as containers saturate before new instances start.

children.title: Preemptible and Spot VMs
children.description: Preemptible (now Spot) VMs cost up to 91% less but can be reclaimed within 30 seconds by GCP. Using Spot VMs for non-fault-tolerant workloads without graceful shutdown handlers results in data corruption and incomplete batch jobs.

children.title: Cloud Functions vs Cloud Run
children.description: Cloud Functions targets single-purpose event handlers with simpler deployment, while Cloud Run supports full containerized services with more control over runtime and concurrency. Choosing Functions for a long-running or high-concurrency workload quickly exhausts execution time limits and increases cost.

gcp-dev.title: Storage and Databases
gcp-dev.description: This layer teaches storing objects and structured data on GCP. It matters because durable storage and the right database choice are central to most applications.
gcp-dev.challenge: Store uploaded files in Cloud Storage and persist application data in Cloud SQL or Firestore.

children.title: Buckets, Objects, and Access Control
children.description: Cloud Storage buckets hold objects with globally unique names; access is controlled via IAM and optionally legacy ACLs. Mixing IAM and ACLs on the same bucket leads to confusing permission behavior where IAM grants are silently overridden by more permissive ACLs.

children.title: Storage Classes and Lifecycle
children.description: GCP Storage offers Standard, Nearline, Coldline, and Archive classes with different retrieval costs and minimum storage durations. Storing infrequently accessed data in Standard class wastes money, while misconfigured lifecycle rules can delete data prematurely.

children.title: Versioning and Retention
children.description: Object versioning keeps previous copies of overwritten or deleted objects, while retention policies enforce minimum holding periods. Without versioning, a misconfigured deployment that overwrites a production object has no recovery path.

children.title: Signed URLs and CORS
children.description: Signed URLs grant time-limited access to Cloud Storage objects without requiring requester authentication, enabling direct browser uploads. Misunderstanding CORS configuration causes browser-side uploads to fail silently with opaque network errors.

children.title: Managed Relational Databases
children.description: Cloud SQL manages MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQL Server with automated backups, patching, and failover replicas. Connecting from Cloud Run or GKE without the Cloud SQL Auth Proxy exposes your database credentials in environment variables and bypasses SSL enforcement.

children.title: High Availability and Replicas
children.description: Cloud SQL HA creates a standby instance in a different zone with automatic failover; read replicas distribute query load. Running a single-instance Cloud SQL setup in production means a zone failure causes downtime that persists until manual intervention.

children.title: Firestore Document Model
children.description: Firestore is a serverless, document-oriented NoSQL database with real-time sync and offline support. Modeling data as deeply nested subcollections without considering query patterns leads to expensive collection group queries or impossible filtering requirements.

children.title: Memorystore for Redis
children.description: Memorystore provides a managed Redis or Memcached instance accessible within your VPC. Skipping a caching layer for a high-read Cloud SQL workload causes repeated expensive queries, and running Redis outside Memorystore forces you to manage patches and failover yourself.

gcp-dev.title: Messaging with Pub/Sub
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers asynchronous, event-driven communication on GCP. It matters because decoupling services with messaging is essential for scalable systems.
gcp-dev.challenge: Wire a Pub/Sub topic that triggers a Cloud Run service to process events asynchronously.

children.title: Custom Mode VPC and Subnets
children.description: Custom VPCs give you full control over IP ranges and subnet placement across regions, unlike auto-mode VPCs which allocate ranges automatically. Using auto-mode VPCs in production makes VPC peering fragile because the fixed /20 ranges frequently collide with partner or on-premises networks.

children.title: Firewall Rules and Tags
children.description: GCP firewall rules use network tags and service account targets to control ingress and egress at the instance level. Relying on allow-all default rules or failing to restrict egress means a compromised VM can exfiltrate data to any destination on the internet.

children.title: Global vs Regional Load Balancers
children.description: GCP's global HTTP(S) Load Balancer uses anycast IPs and routes traffic to the nearest healthy backend globally; regional load balancers stay within a region. Choosing a regional load balancer for a global user base increases latency and eliminates GCP's intelligent traffic management.

children.title: Cloud CDN and SSL Policies
children.description: Cloud CDN caches load-balanced content at Google edge PoPs, reducing origin load and latency; SSL policies enforce TLS version minimums. Not enabling Cloud CDN for a media or static asset workload causes unnecessary backend traffic and higher serving costs.

children.title: VPC Peering and Shared VPC
children.description: VPC Peering connects two VPCs for private routing without transitive paths; Shared VPC centralizes network administration in a host project. Choosing peering when transitive routing is needed forces a full mesh of peering connections that becomes unmanageable at scale.

children.title: Private Google Access
children.description: Private Google Access lets VM instances without external IPs reach Google APIs through internal routing. Disabling Private Google Access on a private subnet forces you to add NAT or external IPs just to call Cloud Storage or Secret Manager, expanding your attack surface.

children.title: Cloud Armor WAF
children.description: Cloud Armor provides DDoS protection and WAF rules enforced at the load balancer edge before requests hit your backends. Without Cloud Armor, SQL injection, XSS, and volumetric DDoS attacks reach your application servers directly with no upstream mitigation layer.

children.title: Secret Manager
children.description: Secret Manager stores, versions, and audits access to API keys, credentials, and certificates. Hardcoding secrets in environment variables or source code is the leading cause of credential leaks in GCP; Secret Manager with Secret Accessor IAM role eliminates the need to embed secrets anywhere.

gcp-dev.title: Networking and Security
gcp-dev.description: This layer deepens VPC networking and access control on GCP. It matters because secure, well-structured networks are required for production workloads.
gcp-dev.challenge: Place your services behind a global load balancer in a VPC with restrictive firewall rules.

children.title: Connection Pooling and Auth Proxy
children.description: The Cloud SQL Auth Proxy establishes encrypted tunnels and authenticates via IAM, removing the need for database password management. Running direct IP connections without the proxy exposes your database to network attacks and requires managing SSL certificates manually.

children.title: Automated Backups and PITR
children.description: Cloud SQL supports automated daily backups and point-in-time recovery (PITR) using binary logging. Disabling PITR means that a database corruption event can only be recovered to the last nightly backup, potentially losing an entire day of transactions.

children.title: Cloud Spanner for Global Scale
children.description: Cloud Spanner provides globally distributed, strongly consistent relational database semantics with horizontal scaling. Using Cloud SQL when you need multi-region writes without downtime forces complex application-level sharding that Spanner handles natively.

children.title: Bigtable for Time-Series and Analytics
children.description: Bigtable is a petabyte-scale NoSQL database optimized for time-series, IoT telemetry, and high-throughput key-value access patterns. Attempting to run high-cardinality time-series data in Firestore or Cloud SQL causes scan performance degradation that Bigtable's column-family design avoids entirely.

children.title: IAM Database Authentication
children.description: Cloud SQL supports IAM-based authentication so Google accounts and service accounts can log in without passwords. Without IAM auth, rotating shared database passwords becomes a manual, error-prone coordination exercise across every service that connects to the database.

children.title: Private IP and VPC Service Controls
children.description: Configuring Cloud SQL with private IP keeps all traffic within the VPC; VPC Service Controls add a perimeter to prevent data exfiltration across API calls. Leaving Cloud SQL on a public IP with authorized networks is a defense-in-depth failure even if IP whitelisting is applied correctly.

children.title: Database Migration Service
children.description: Database Migration Service (DMS) handles continuous replication from on-premises or other cloud databases into Cloud SQL or AlloyDB with minimal downtime. Performing a large migration with pg_dump and manual import causes multi-hour downtime windows that DMS change-data-capture eliminates.

children.title: Datastream for CDC
children.description: Datastream provides serverless change data capture and replication from operational databases into BigQuery or Cloud Storage. Without CDC, building analytical views of transactional data requires full table exports that introduce latency and lock contention on the source database.

gcp-dev.title: Data Analytics with BigQuery
gcp-dev.description: This layer introduces large-scale analytics and data warehousing. It matters because BigQuery enables fast analysis over huge datasets without managing infrastructure.
gcp-dev.challenge: Stream events from Pub/Sub into BigQuery and run analytical queries over the collected data.

children.title: Triggers and Event Sources
children.description: Cloud Functions (2nd gen) can be triggered by HTTP, Pub/Sub, Cloud Storage events, Firestore changes, and Eventarc. Using Pub/Sub push triggers without configuring dead-letter topics means failed function invocations silently drop events after maximum retries are exhausted.

children.title: Function Concurrency and Limits
children.description: Cloud Functions 2nd gen supports per-instance concurrency similar to Cloud Run, unlike 1st gen which handled one request per instance. Not understanding the 540-second maximum execution time limit causes long-running data processing functions to terminate midway without surfacing an error to the caller.

children.title: Eventarc for Unified Event Routing
children.description: Eventarc routes events from Google Cloud services, Pub/Sub, and third-party sources to Cloud Run, Cloud Functions, or Workflows using CloudEvents format. Bypassing Eventarc with direct Pub/Sub subscriptions per service creates a tightly coupled fan-out pattern that breaks when adding new consumers.

children.title: Cloud Workflows for Orchestration
children.description: Cloud Workflows orchestrates sequences of HTTP-based API calls with built-in retries, error handling, and conditional branching. Implementing multi-step serverless workflows as chained Pub/Sub events produces invisible state machines that are impossible to monitor, debug, or resume after failures.

children.title: Topics, Subscriptions, and Delivery
children.description: Pub/Sub guarantees at-least-once delivery; your subscribers must be idempotent because the same message can be delivered more than once. Building non-idempotent consumers on Pub/Sub causes duplicate database writes, double-charged payments, or repeated notifications during retry storms.

children.title: Dead-Letter Topics and Retry Policies
children.description: Dead-letter topics capture messages that exceed the maximum delivery attempts so they can be inspected and reprocessed without loss. Without a dead-letter topic, poison messages that always fail processing block subscription throughput and are silently discarded after retry exhaustion.

children.title: Function Identity and IAM
children.description: Cloud Functions runs with a service account identity that should be granted only the IAM roles it needs. Using the default App Engine service account for all functions grants Editor-level access to your project, meaning any function can read or overwrite any resource.

children.title: VPC Connector for Private Connectivity
children.description: Serverless VPC Access connectors allow Cloud Functions and Cloud Run to reach private VPC resources like Memorystore or Cloud SQL on private IPs. Without a VPC connector, serverless workloads must use public IPs for database connections, exposing internal services to the internet.

gcp-dev.title: Kubernetes with GKE
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers running containerized workloads on managed Kubernetes. It matters because GKE is the standard way to run complex, scalable container platforms on GCP.
gcp-dev.challenge: Deploy a multi-service application to a GKE cluster with autoscaling node pools and Ingress.

children.title: Autopilot vs Standard Mode
children.description: GKE Autopilot manages node provisioning and scaling automatically and bills per Pod resource request, while Standard mode gives full control over node pools. Choosing Standard when you lack node management expertise leads to perpetually over-provisioned clusters that cost significantly more than Autopilot equivalents.

children.title: Node Pools and Autoscaling
children.description: Node pools group VMs of the same type within a cluster; cluster autoscaler adds or removes nodes based on pending Pod demands. Running a single node pool for mixed workloads prevents efficient bin-packing — GPU nodes sit idle during CPU-only periods because the autoscaler cannot remove them.

children.title: Workload Identity Federation
children.description: Workload Identity binds a Kubernetes service account to a GCP service account, so Pods can call GCP APIs without JSON key files. Distributing GCP service account JSON keys as Kubernetes Secrets creates long-lived credentials that are difficult to rotate and expose your project if a Secret is leaked.

children.title: GKE RBAC and Network Policies
children.description: GKE integrates Kubernetes RBAC with Google IAM for cluster access control; Network Policies enforce Pod-to-Pod traffic rules. Without Network Policies, every Pod in your cluster can reach every other Pod, meaning a compromised microservice can freely access your database Pods.

children.title: GKE Ingress and Gateway API
children.description: GKE Ingress provisions a Google Cloud HTTP(S) Load Balancer and maps hostnames and paths to Services. Using NodePort Services with manual load balancers instead of GKE Ingress forces you to manage health checks, SSL termination, and global routing yourself — losing the tight GCP integration.

children.title: Container-Native Load Balancing
children.description: Container-native load balancing routes traffic directly to Pod IPs via Network Endpoint Groups (NEGs) instead of Node IPs, reducing latency and improving health check granularity. Without NEGs, traffic routes through kube-proxy on nodes, adding a hop and preventing the load balancer from detecting individual failing Pods.

children.title: GKE Cluster Upgrades
children.description: GKE supports rolling control plane upgrades and node pool surge upgrades with configurable disruption budgets. Skipping minor version upgrades causes the cluster to fall outside the GKE support window, at which point Google no longer provides security patches or bug fixes.

children.title: GKE Autopilot Cost Optimization
children.description: Autopilot charges only for requested CPU, memory, and ephemeral storage per Pod — not for nodes — making right-sizing critical for cost control. Setting resource requests far above actual usage in Autopilot is more costly than in Standard mode because every requested millicpu is billed even when idle.

gcp-dev.title: Observability and Operations
gcp-dev.description: This layer teaches monitoring, logging, and tracing GCP workloads. It matters because operating services reliably requires deep visibility into their behavior.
gcp-dev.challenge: Add monitoring dashboards, alerting policies, and tracing to your Cloud Run and GKE services.

children.title: Google Provider and Resource Graph
children.description: The Terraform Google provider maps HCL resources to GCP API calls and tracks state so changes are incremental. Failing to pin the provider version causes unexpected breaking changes when a new provider release renames or removes arguments, silently destroying resources on the next apply.

children.title: Remote State with GCS
children.description: Storing Terraform state in a Cloud Storage bucket with versioning and state locking via Terraform Cloud or a custom lock ensures team collaboration without state corruption. Using local state files in a team environment means concurrent applies overwrite each other's changes, leading to infrastructure drift.

children.title: GCP Deployment Manager Templates
children.description: Deployment Manager uses YAML or Jinja2/Python templates to declare GCP resources and manages them as a deployment stack. Because Deployment Manager is GCP-native, it cannot provision multi-cloud resources — teams adopting hybrid cloud should evaluate Terraform before investing in Deployment Manager templates.

children.title: Config Connector for Kubernetes-Native IaC
children.description: Config Connector lets you manage GCP resources as Kubernetes custom resources, enabling GitOps workflows where your GCP infrastructure is declared alongside your Kubernetes manifests. Ignoring Config Connector when already using GKE and ArgoCD means maintaining two separate IaC toolchains where one would suffice.

children.title: Cloud Build Pipelines
children.description: Cloud Build executes build steps as Docker containers on Google-managed infrastructure, integrating natively with Artifact Registry and Cloud Deploy. Not using substitution variables for environment-specific configuration forces you to maintain separate cloudbuild.yaml files for each environment instead of a single parameterized pipeline.

children.title: Cloud Deploy for Progressive Delivery
children.description: Cloud Deploy manages promotion through a delivery pipeline of targets (dev, staging, prod) with optional manual approvals and rollback. Skipping Cloud Deploy and scripting kubectl apply directly in Cloud Build bypasses audit trails, rollback capabilities, and the approval gates that prevent accidental production deploys.

children.title: Container and Package Repositories
children.description: Artifact Registry is the successor to Container Registry and supports Docker, Maven, npm, Python, and Helm repositories with fine-grained IAM access. Using Container Registry instead of Artifact Registry means missing CMEK encryption support, VPC Service Controls integration, and the multi-format repository model.

children.title: Vulnerability Scanning and Binary Authorization
children.description: Artifact Registry integrates with Container Analysis for on-push vulnerability scanning, and Binary Authorization enforces that only signed images from trusted registries are deployed to GKE. Deploying images without scanning allows known CVEs to reach production, and without Binary Authorization nothing prevents a developer from deploying an unreviewed image.

gcp-dev.title: Infrastructure as Code and CI/CD
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers automating GCP infrastructure and deployments. It matters because reproducible infrastructure and pipelines are required for safe, fast delivery.
topics: Deploying to Cloud Run and GKE
gcp-dev.challenge: Provision your GCP resources with Terraform and deploy them via a Cloud Build pipeline on each commit.

children.title: Metrics, Alerting Policies, and SLOs
children.description: Cloud Monitoring collects built-in and custom metrics and lets you define alerting policies with notification channels (email, PagerDuty, Slack). Without alerting policies aligned to your SLO burn rate, you discover service degradation through customer complaints rather than automated pages.

children.title: Uptime Checks and Dashboards
children.description: Cloud Monitoring uptime checks send synthetic probes from multiple global locations and alert when your endpoint becomes unreachable. Relying solely on internal health checks misses scenarios where DNS, load balancer, or CDN issues prevent external users from reaching your service even when internal probes succeed.

children.title: Log Sinks and Retention
children.description: Cloud Logging retains logs for 30 days by default; log sinks route logs to Cloud Storage, BigQuery, or Pub/Sub for longer retention or analysis. Relying on default 30-day retention makes forensic investigation after a security incident impossible if the breach was not detected immediately.

children.title: Log-Based Metrics and Alerts
children.description: You can create Cloud Monitoring metrics from log entry patterns, enabling alerts triggered by log content such as error keywords or specific HTTP status codes. Without log-based metrics, detecting application-level errors requires sampling logs manually rather than receiving automated alerts the moment error rates spike.

children.title: Cloud Trace
children.description: Cloud Trace collects distributed tracing data from GCP-managed services and OpenTelemetry-instrumented applications to show end-to-end request latency. Without tracing, diagnosing a slow API response requires guessing which microservice or database call is responsible rather than following a visual flame graph to the root cause.

children.title: Cloud Profiler
children.description: Cloud Profiler captures continuous CPU and heap profiles from production applications with minimal overhead, surfacing which code paths consume the most resources. Without profiler data, optimizing performance requires guesswork from load test results rather than evidence from actual production traffic.

children.title: Error Reporting
children.description: Cloud Error Reporting automatically groups, deduplicates, and alerts on application exceptions reported through Cloud Logging or the Error Reporting API. Without Error Reporting, new exception types introduced by a deployment go unnoticed until enough users complain to justify investigation.

children.title: Cloud Audit Logs
children.description: Cloud Audit Logs record Admin Activity, Data Access, and System Event logs for every GCP API call, providing an immutable trail for compliance and forensics. Disabling Data Access audit logs for Cloud SQL or Cloud Storage means you cannot determine which service account read or modified sensitive data during an incident.

gcp-dev.title: Production Architecture and Scaling
gcp-dev.description: This layer covers running well-architected, scalable systems in production on GCP. It matters because production systems must balance reliability, cost, and security under real load.
gcp-dev.challenge: Architect and deploy a multi-region, highly available version of your application reviewed against the architecture framework.

children.title: Billing Export and Cost Analysis
children.description: Exporting detailed billing data to BigQuery enables custom queries, dashboards, and programmatic cost attribution by label, service, and SKU. Without billing export, you are limited to the Cloud Console's pre-built reports and cannot correlate cost spikes with specific deployments or teams.

children.title: Committed Use Discounts
children.description: GCP Committed Use Discounts (CUDs) offer up to 57% off on-demand prices for 1- or 3-year commitments to specific vCPU and memory amounts. Running steady-state workloads on on-demand pricing when usage is predictable is one of the most common sources of unnecessary GCP spend.

children.title: Cost Allocation with Labels
children.description: GCP labels are key-value pairs attached to resources and reflected in billing exports, enabling per-team, per-environment, or per-application cost breakdown. Without consistent labeling enforced via Organization Policies, billing data becomes unattributable and FinOps chargebacks are impossible to calculate.

children.title: Budget Alerts and Quota Management
children.description: Cloud Billing budgets trigger Pub/Sub notifications or email alerts when spend reaches defined thresholds, allowing automated responses like disabling APIs. Without budget alerts, runaway infrastructure from a misconfigured autoscaler or forgotten test environment accumulates charges that are only discovered at month-end billing.

children.title: Well-Architected Pillars on GCP
children.description: The Google Cloud Architecture Framework defines six pillars — operational excellence, security, reliability, cost optimization, performance, and sustainability — that guide architectural decisions. Designing systems without consulting the framework produces architectures that satisfy one pillar (usually functionality) while failing the others.

children.title: Multi-Region High Availability
children.description: GCP supports active-active multi-region architectures using global load balancers, multi-region Cloud Storage, Spanner, and Firestore. Designing a single-region active-passive DR strategy without testing failover procedures results in Recovery Time Objectives that are far longer than planned when an actual regional event occurs.

children.title: Organization Policies
children.description: Organization Policies enforce constraints across all projects in your GCP organization, such as restricting resource locations to specific regions or disabling public IPs. Without Organization Policies, individual developers can create resources in any region or with public IPs, violating data residency requirements and security baselines.

children.title: Carbon Footprint and Sustainability
children.description: GCP's Carbon Footprint tool reports gross and net carbon emissions per product, project, and region, supporting ESG reporting requirements. Ignoring sustainability metrics means missing the opportunity to shift workloads to lower-carbon regions or carbon-free energy time slots that GCP makes accessible without performance trade-offs.

azure-dev.title: Azure Cloud Fundamentals
azure-dev.description: This layer introduces the Azure account structure and identity model. It matters because subscriptions, resource groups, and identity govern everything you build on Azure.
azure-dev.challenge: Create a resource group and assign RBAC roles, then manage resources through the Azure CLI.

azure-dev.title: Compute and App Services
azure-dev.description: This layer covers Azure compute options from virtual machines to serverless. It matters because selecting the right hosting model shapes scalability and operational cost.
azure-dev.challenge: Deploy a web application to App Service and add an Azure Function triggered by an HTTP request.

azure-dev.title: Storage and Databases
azure-dev.description: This layer teaches storing files and structured data on Azure. It matters because durable storage and appropriate database choices are central to most applications.
azure-dev.challenge: Store uploads in Blob Storage and persist application data in Azure SQL or Cosmos DB.

azure-dev.title: Serverless with Azure Functions
azure-dev.description: This layer covers building event-driven serverless solutions on Azure. It matters because serverless lowers operational overhead and is a core modern Azure pattern.
azure-dev.challenge: Build an event-driven workflow where a Service Bus message triggers an Azure Function to process data.

azure-dev.title: Networking and Security
azure-dev.description: This layer deepens Azure networking and access control. It matters because secure, segmented networks and managed secrets are required for production workloads.
azure-dev.challenge: Place your app behind an Application Gateway in a virtual network and load its secrets from Key Vault.

azure-dev.title: Infrastructure as Code with Bicep
azure-dev.description: This layer teaches declaring Azure infrastructure with Bicep. It matters because reproducible, version-controlled infrastructure is essential for reliable Azure environments.
azure-dev.challenge: Reprovision your compute, storage, and networking resources entirely through a Bicep template.

azure-dev.title: Kubernetes with AKS
azure-dev.description: This layer covers running containerized workloads on managed Kubernetes. It matters because AKS is the standard way to run scalable container platforms on Azure.
azure-dev.challenge: Deploy a multi-service application to an AKS cluster with autoscaling node pools and Ingress.

azure-dev.title: Observability and Operations
azure-dev.description: This layer teaches monitoring, logging, and tracing Azure workloads. It matters because visibility into systems is required to operate them reliably.
azure-dev.challenge: Add Application Insights and Azure Monitor alerts to your services with a Log Analytics dashboard.

azure-dev.title: CI/CD with Azure DevOps
azure-dev.description: This layer covers automating builds and deployments with Azure DevOps. It matters because automated delivery is what makes cloud iteration fast and reliable.
azure-dev.challenge: Build an Azure Pipeline that tests and deploys your Bicep-defined infrastructure on every commit.

azure-dev.title: Production Architecture and Scaling
azure-dev.description: This layer covers running well-architected, scalable systems in production on Azure. It matters because production systems must balance reliability, cost, and security under real load.
azure-dev.challenge: Architect and deploy a multi-region, highly available version of your application reviewed against the Well-Architected pillars.

devsecops.title: Security Fundamentals for DevOps
devsecops.description: This layer introduces core security concepts within a DevOps workflow. It matters because shifting security left starts with understanding threats, risk, and the secure development lifecycle.
devsecops.challenge: Build a threat model for a sample web application and document its top risks and mitigations.

devsecops.title: Dependency and SCA Scanning with Snyk
devsecops.description: This layer covers finding vulnerabilities in third-party dependencies. It matters because most modern applications inherit risk from open-source libraries.
devsecops.challenge: Scan a project with Snyk and remediate the highest severity dependency vulnerabilities it reports.

devsecops.title: Static Analysis with SonarQube
devsecops.description: This layer teaches scanning source code for bugs and security flaws. It matters because static analysis catches insecure code before it ever runs.
devsecops.challenge: Add a SonarQube quality gate to a pipeline that fails the build on new security hotspots.

devsecops.title: Container and Image Security with Trivy
devsecops.description: This layer covers scanning container images and configurations for vulnerabilities. It matters because containers can ship known vulnerabilities and misconfigurations into production.
devsecops.challenge: Scan your container images and IaC with Trivy and harden the base image to remove critical findings.

devsecops.title: Secrets Management with Vault
devsecops.description: This layer teaches storing and distributing secrets securely. It matters because hardcoded credentials are a frequent and dangerous source of breaches.
devsecops.challenge: Use Vault to issue dynamic database credentials and inject them into a running application.

devsecops.title: Secure CI/CD Pipelines
devsecops.description: This layer covers hardening the delivery pipeline itself. It matters because pipelines are a high-value target and a single weak step can compromise everything.
devsecops.challenge: Add SCA, SAST, and image scanning gates to a pipeline and replace static credentials with OIDC.

devsecops.title: Dynamic Testing and Runtime Security
devsecops.description: This layer covers testing running applications and detecting threats at runtime. It matters because some vulnerabilities only appear when systems are actually executing.
devsecops.challenge: Run a ZAP scan against your deployed app and configure Falco to alert on suspicious runtime behavior.

devsecops.title: Policy as Code and Compliance
devsecops.description: This layer teaches enforcing security and compliance rules automatically. It matters because manual policy enforcement does not scale and is easily bypassed.
devsecops.challenge: Write OPA policies that block non-compliant Terraform plans and insecure Kubernetes manifests.

devsecops.title: Supply Chain Security
devsecops.description: This layer covers securing the software supply chain end to end. It matters because attackers increasingly target build systems and dependencies rather than running apps.
devsecops.challenge: Sign your container images with Sigstore and enforce signature verification before deployment.

devsecops.title: Security Operations at Scale
devsecops.description: This layer covers running security operations across a production organization. It matters because security must be continuous, measurable, and integrated into daily operations at scale.
devsecops.challenge: Integrate scanning, runtime alerts, and signing into a unified security dashboard with an incident playbook.

platform-engineer.title: Platform Engineering Foundations
platform-engineer.description: This layer introduces the goals and mindset of internal developer platforms. It matters because platform engineering aims to reduce cognitive load and accelerate every developer in the organization.
topics: What an internal developer platform is
platform-engineer.challenge: Write a one-page vision for an internal platform defining its first golden path for developers.

platform-engineer.title: Kubernetes as a Platform Base
platform-engineer.description: This layer establishes Kubernetes as the runtime substrate for the platform. It matters because most internal platforms build their abstractions on top of Kubernetes.
platform-engineer.challenge: Provision a shared cluster with isolated namespaces and quotas for two sample tenant teams.

platform-engineer.title: Infrastructure as Code for Platforms
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers provisioning platform infrastructure declaratively. It matters because the platform itself must be reproducible, versioned, and reviewable.
platform-engineer.challenge: Build reusable Terraform modules that provision a standard environment for a new tenant team.

platform-engineer.title: Developer Portals with Backstage
platform-engineer.description: This layer introduces building a developer portal as the platform front door. It matters because a portal centralizes discovery, documentation, and self-service for developers.
platform-engineer.challenge: Stand up a Backstage instance and register your sample services in the software catalog.

platform-engineer.title: Golden Paths and Templates
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers building paved roads that let developers create services quickly. It matters because templated golden paths encode best practices and reduce setup friction.
platform-engineer.challenge: Create a Backstage template that scaffolds a new service with CI/CD and catalog registration built in.

platform-engineer.title: GitOps Delivery with ArgoCD
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers delivering platform and tenant workloads through GitOps. It matters because GitOps gives the platform auditable, self-healing, declarative deployments.

resources.label: TechWorld with Nana GitOps with ArgoCD

platform-engineer.challenge: Deliver tenant applications through ArgoCD using an app of apps pattern driven from Git.

platform-engineer.title: Self-Service APIs and Operators
platform-engineer.description: This layer teaches exposing infrastructure as self-service Kubernetes APIs. It matters because custom abstractions let developers request resources without learning the underlying tools.
platform-engineer.challenge: Use Crossplane to expose a self-service API that lets developers provision a database with one manifest.

platform-engineer.title: Observability and Golden Signals
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers building observability into the platform for all tenants. It matters because a platform must give every team consistent visibility without each reinventing it.
platform-engineer.challenge: Provide a default observability stack so any service onboarded to the platform gets dashboards automatically.

platform-engineer.title: Policy, Security, and Governance
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers enforcing standards and security across the platform. It matters because platforms must keep all tenants compliant and secure without slowing them down.
platform-engineer.challenge: Add platform guardrails with OPA policies and per-tenant secrets so teams stay secure by default.

platform-engineer.title: Operating the Platform at Scale
platform-engineer.description: This layer covers running and evolving the platform as a product in production. It matters because a platform must scale to many teams while staying reliable, adopted, and continuously improved.
platform-engineer.challenge: Operate the full platform end to end, onboard multiple teams through golden paths, and measure adoption.
